5.4.a.info:
Password complexity means requiring a password to contain characters drawn from multiple character classes — uppercase letters, lowercase letters, digits, and special symbols. The idea is to enlarge the keyspace an attacker must search during a brute-force or dictionary attack.

The four classic character classes (mnemonic: ULNS):

  • Uppercase (A–Z) — 26 possibilities
  • Lowercase (a–z) — 26 possibilities
  • Numbers (0–9) — 10 possibilities
  • Special symbols (!@#$%^&*...) — roughly 32 possibilities

With all four classes in play, each character position has ~94 possible values, so a 12-char password offers ~94^12 (~4.75 × 10^23) combinations. That is astronomically large — but complexity alone is not enough. Users faced with complexity rules tend to pick predictable patterns like P@ssw0rd1!, which attackers can crack in seconds using tailored wordlists.

What IOS can and can't enforce: Cisco IOS can enforce a minimum length with security passwords min-length [0-16], but it has no built-in knob to require a mix of character classes. True complexity enforcement lives on a AAA server (Cisco ISE / RADIUS / TACACS+) or the OS handling the credential. This is a favorite exam trap — the switch cannot force upper+lower+digit+symbol on its own.

Commands: security passwords min-length 10 (global config), service password-encryption (weak type-7 obfuscation of clear-text passwords), enable secret (stores as type-8/9 hash). Length rules apply to new passwords, not existing ones.

left.label: Strong complexity
items: Mix of U + L + N + S

hack.memory: ULNS = Uppercase, Lowercase, Numbers, Symbols. Four classes = big keyspace. But P@ssw0rd is still weak — attackers know the substitution tricks. IOS enforces length only; complexity rules live on a AAA server.
hack.practice: On a Cisco switch: `security passwords min-length 10`. Try setting a user password of 'abc' — IOS rejects it. Then try 'abcdefghij' — accepted (meets length) even though it has no complexity. That single test proves IOS enforces length, not complexity.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. The exam often gives a password example and asks whether it meets a stated policy. Read the policy carefully (minimum length? required classes?) and compare character by character. Remember: IOS = length only; AAA = length + complexity.

5.4.a.info:
Password length is the single most important variable in password strength. Every additional character multiplies the keyspace by the size of the character set (~94 for a full mixed set). Attackers brute-force shorter passwords in hours; a well-chosen longer passphrase can take centuries on the same hardware.

Why length beats complexity: A 16-character passphrase like correct-horse-battery-staple has far more entropy than an 8-character complex password like P@ssw0rd. Humans remember the phrase; computers struggle to crack it. Complexity rules often backfire — they push users to predictable substitutions (o→0, a→@) that attackers explicitly model.

Modern length benchmarks:

  • Minimum: 12 characters for user accounts, 15+ for privileged accounts.
  • NIST SP 800-63B (current): allow up to 64 characters, accept all printable ASCII + spaces, encourage passphrases.
  • Cisco enable secret: no documented upper bound but practical limits apply; 25+ characters is a reasonable target for infra devices.

IOS enforcement: security passwords min-length [0-16] sets a floor on new password length globally. It does NOT retroactively invalidate existing shorter passwords. After the command, the next time anyone changes a password (enable secret, username x secret y, line passwords, etc.), the minimum is enforced.

Exam trap: the command is security passwords min-length, not password min-length. The max argument is 16 — even though longer passwords are desirable in practice, the IOS knob caps at 16. Real-world security best practice goes well past the IOS floor.

hack.memory: Length is king. Every extra character multiplies keyspace by ~94. A 20-char passphrase beats an 8-char 'complex' password by orders of magnitude. IOS knob: `security passwords min-length [0-16]`, max value 16. Real infra minimum target: 15+.
hack.practice: On a Cisco router, set `security passwords min-length 10`. Then try `enable secret abc123` (7 chars) — rejected. Try `enable secret MyStrongP@ss9` (13 chars) — accepted. Compare crack-time estimates on howsecureismypassword.net: 8-char complex vs 16-char passphrase — the longer one wins by trillions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Know the exact command syntax and the 0-16 range. Know that length > complexity is the modern guidance. On the exam, if you see a passphrase vs a short complex password, the passphrase is stronger.

5.4.a.info:
NIST Special Publication 800-63B is the US federal standard for digital identity authentication. The 2017 revision (updated 2020 and 2024) significantly reshaped password guidance, moving away from decades-old 'complexity + rotation' dogma toward length, passphrases, and breach-informed checks.

Key NIST recommendations:

  • Allow long passwords — minimum 8, maximum at least 64 characters. Accept all printable ASCII and spaces.
  • Do NOT force periodic rotation — change only on evidence of compromise. Forced rotation pushes users to weak patterns (Spring2024! → Summer2024!).
  • Do NOT require specific character classes — complexity rules create predictable patterns.
  • Do check against breach lists — reject passwords found in known breach corpuses (haveibeenpwned, etc.).
  • Do NOT use password hints or security questions as sole recovery factors — they are 'something you know' and often publicly discoverable.
  • Rate-limit login attempts to blunt online brute force.

Why the shift? Real-world data showed forced rotation + complexity drove users to weaker, more predictable credentials. Long passphrases plus breach-list checks catch more actual attacks. Modern IAM systems (Azure AD, Okta, Google Workspace) already implement this model.

For CCNA: the exam may still test older 'rotate every 60-90 days' policies because many compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, some HIPAA guidance) lag the research. Know both the traditional view and the modern NIST view — pick the answer that matches the framing of the question.

hack.memory: NIST 2017+ flipped the script: length > complexity, no forced rotation, check breach lists. The old 'Password1 → Password2' rotation policy actively made things worse. For the exam, know BOTH views — older content still tests 90-day rotation.
hack.practice: Read NIST SP 800-63B section 5.1.1.2 (memorized secrets). Then compare to your company's password policy — count the gaps. Is rotation forced? Is breach-list checking enabled? Draft a modern policy using NIST as the template.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tip: when a question frames the policy as 'best practice modern,' answer NIST. When framed as 'traditional' or 'compliance-driven,' answer rotation + complexity. Read the stem carefully.

5.4.b.info:
Password rotation is the traditional policy of forcing users to change passwords on a fixed schedule — most commonly every 60 or 90 days. The rationale: if a password is silently compromised, rotation limits the window of exposure.

How it's implemented:

  • AAA server / Active Directory — password-max-age policy set in days. At expiration, the user is forced to change at next login.
  • Cisco IOS — supports aging via AAA integration; local IOS user accounts have limited built-in aging (enable via username x privilege y secret z with AAA policy or use external RADIUS/TACACS+).
  • Compliance drivers — PCI-DSS historically required 90-day rotation; many internal audit frameworks still do.

Why modern guidance pushes back: Empirical studies (Carleton, UNC 2010+, FTC 2016) showed forced rotation drives predictable transforms (Password1! → Password2! → Password3!). Attackers who steal one version can often guess the next. NIST SP 800-63B (2017) explicitly recommends against time-based expiration unless compromise is suspected.

Still-valid use cases for rotation: shared service accounts, emergency break-glass credentials, accounts without MFA, and regulated environments where compliance mandates it. For individual user accounts with strong passwords + MFA, forced rotation hurts more than it helps.

CCNA exam framing: expect questions that describe a traditional 'rotate every 90 days, keep history of last 12, lock out after 5 failures' policy. These are baseline compliance controls the exam tests. Just also recognize the newer NIST view when it appears.

hack.memory: 90-day rotation = 'old school' policy. Still widely enforced by compliance (PCI, HIPAA legacy) but modern research and NIST say rotation without cause creates weaker passwords, not stronger ones. Know both views.
hack.practice: Audit a password policy you actually use (work AD, Gmail, bank). How often does each force rotation? Which still force 90-day expiration? Which allow 'never expires' with MFA? Real-world calibration beats textbook theory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam can frame this either way. Traditional rotation = 60/90 days. Modern NIST = change on compromise only. The '90-day' answer is still correct on compliance-flavored questions.

5.4.b.info:
Password history is the policy of remembering a user's past N passwords and blocking reuse of any of them. Common setting: remember last 10-24 passwords. Without this control, a user forced to rotate would simply cycle back to a favorite password after enough changes.

How it works:

  • When a user changes their password, the system hashes the new value and compares against stored hashes of the last N passwords.
  • If a match is found, the change is rejected with 'password has been used recently.'
  • Old hashes are discarded once they fall off the end of the history window.

Interaction with rotation: history + rotation together form the classic compliance pairing. Rotation every 90 days + history of last 12 means a user cannot return to an old password for at least 3 years (12 × 90 days). In practice, users defeat this by incrementing a suffix (Password1 → Password2 → ... → Password13).

Where it's enforced:

  • Active Directory — 'Enforce password history' group policy (default 24 on Windows Server).
  • Linux — PAM module pam_pwhistory with remember=N.
  • AAA servers (ISE, TACACS+) — per-policy history depth.
  • Cisco IOS local accounts — no native history control; rely on AAA server.

Common value: history of 10-24 is standard. PCI-DSS 4.0 requires at least 4. NIST SP 800-63B doesn't mandate history because it deprioritizes rotation entirely.

hack.memory: History = 'remember the last N, can't reuse them.' Standard: 10-24. Usually paired with rotation in compliance frameworks. Cisco IOS locally = no native history; use AAA server.
hack.practice: On a Windows domain, check Default Domain Policy → Account Policies → Password Policy → Enforce password history. On Linux: grep pam_pwhistory /etc/pam.d/system-auth. Observe the default N. In both systems, try to set your password back to a recent value — it's rejected.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam may ask 'what prevents a user from cycling back to an old password?' = password history. Know that IOS relies on AAA for this and doesn't enforce history locally.

5.4.b.info:
Account lockout disables a user account after a configurable number of consecutive failed login attempts. It is the primary defense against online brute-force attacks — where an attacker tries password guesses against a live login prompt.

Key knobs:

  • Threshold — number of failures before lockout (common: 3-10).
  • Duration — how long the account stays locked (common: 15-30 minutes, or until admin unlock).
  • Counter reset — how long of successful-login silence resets the counter.

On Cisco IOS — login block-for: the feature is login block-for [seconds] attempts [N] within [seconds]. This blocks all login attempts on VTY lines (not individual accounts) for the configured duration after too many failures — protecting the device from brute-force without locking out specific users.

login block-for 120 attempts 5 within 60
! blocks all logins for 120s after 5 failed attempts in 60s

Additional IOS commands:

  • login quiet-mode access-class [ACL] — allows permitted sources to still log in during quiet mode.
  • login delay [seconds] — inserts a delay between each login attempt, slowing online brute force.
  • login on-failure log / login on-success log — send syslog events.

Trade-off: lockout can cause self-inflicted DoS. An attacker intentionally failing logins can lock out legitimate accounts. Mitigations: separate IP-based rate limiting from account lockout; use account unlocking via self-service + MFA; monitor lockout events in SIEM.

hack.memory: Lockout = blunt brute-force defense. IOS command: `login block-for SECONDS attempts N within SECONDS`. Blocks VTY logins device-wide, not per-account. Risk: attacker can weaponize lockout to DoS legit users.
hack.practice: On a Cisco router in a lab: `login block-for 60 attempts 3 within 30`. Then SSH with wrong passwords 3 times in 30s. Try to log in legitimately — blocked for 60s. This makes the mechanism concrete.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam can ask for the exact command. Memorize: `login block-for [secs] attempts [N] within [secs]`. Also know this applies to VTY/console, not per-user lockout (that needs AAA).

5.4.c.info:
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) requires a user to present two or more authentication factors drawn from different categories to prove identity. The strength of MFA comes from the independence of the factors — compromising one does not help an attacker compromise another.

The three canonical factor categories:

  • Something you KNOW — password, PIN, passphrase, security question answer. Knowledge-based. Can be phished, shoulder-surfed, or guessed.
  • Something you HAVE — smartphone (for TOTP or push), hardware token (YubiKey, RSA SecurID), smart card, certificate on a device. Possession-based. Must be physically stolen or its secret extracted.
  • Something you ARE — biometrics: fingerprint, face, iris, voice, retina. Inherence-based. Hardest to forge but cannot be re-issued if the template is stolen.

Sometimes also counted (context factors): somewhere you are (geolocation, IP geofencing), something you do (behavioral — keystroke cadence, mouse movement, gait). Modern risk-based authentication blends these.

Real MFA combinations: password (Know) + phone TOTP (Have); smart card (Have) + PIN (Know); password (Know) + fingerprint (Are); badge tap (Have) + iris scan (Are). Each pair forces the attacker to compromise fundamentally different systems.

CCNA exam emphasis: the exam repeatedly tests the 'different categories' requirement. Two passwords, password + security question, password + PIN — none of these are MFA, because both factors are in the Know category. This is the single most common MFA trap.

hack.memory: MFA = factors from DIFFERENT categories. KHA: Know, Have, Are. Password + phone OTP = real MFA. Password + security question = NOT MFA (both Know). Two fingerprints = NOT MFA (both Are).
hack.practice: Classify 10 auth combos rapidly. Password + SMS code (Know + Have = MFA). PIN + password (Know + Know = NOT MFA). Fingerprint + face (Are + Are = NOT MFA). Smart card + PIN (Have + Know = MFA). Speed builds exam reflex.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Guaranteed exam question. Always test: are the two factors in different categories? If yes, MFA. If no, just two-step, not true MFA.

5.4.c.info:
The 'Something you KNOW' factor is the oldest and most common authentication factor. It is any secret the user remembers and presents at login. Because it's knowledge-based, it can be transferred (whispered, written down, phished) without the user losing physical possession.

Examples: passwords, PINs, passphrases, pattern locks, security question answers ('mother's maiden name'), one-time paper codes a user has memorized.

Strengths: cheap to deploy, no hardware, familiar to every user. Can be changed after compromise (unlike biometrics).

Weaknesses:

  • Phishable — a convincing fake login page harvests the secret directly.
  • Shoulder-surfable — anyone watching the keyboard or screen can capture it.
  • Guessable — users pick predictable values (pet names, birthdays, Password1).
  • Reused — users recycle the same password across sites, so one breach compromises many accounts.

Security questions are particularly weak because the answers are often publicly discoverable (social media, public records) and are still 'Know' factors — so combining a password with a security question is not MFA.

On Cisco devices: the enable secret, line passwords, and local usernames are all Know-factor credentials. To add a second factor for device login, integrate the device with a AAA server (Cisco ISE) that can enforce push/TOTP alongside the password.

hack.memory: Know = stuff in your brain. Passwords, PINs, secret answers. Easy to deploy, easy to phish. Security questions are 'Know' too — pairing them with a password is NOT MFA.
hack.practice: List every 'Know' credential you use daily: passwords, PINs, unlock patterns, pet name questions. Ask: how many of these are derivable from your public social media or a 10-minute LinkedIn search? That's your attack surface.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. The exam lists items and asks you to categorize them. PIN, password, security question answer, passphrase = all Know. Memorize the list.

5.4.c.info:
The 'Something you HAVE' factor is a physical or digital object the user possesses and must present at login. Compromising this factor requires stealing or cloning the object — meaningfully harder than harvesting a password.

Examples:

  • Smartphone with authenticator app — generates TOTP codes (Google Authenticator, Authy, Microsoft Authenticator) or receives push approvals.
  • Hardware OTP tokens — RSA SecurID fob, YubiKey Token with rolling code.
  • FIDO2 / U2F security keys — USB or NFC devices like YubiKey, Titan Key. Phishing-resistant because the key checks the exact domain before signing.
  • Smart cards / PIV cards — carry a certificate and private key; tap or insert to authenticate.
  • SIM card (for SMS OTP) — technically 'Have' but weak because SIMs can be swapped socially-engineered out of carriers.

TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password, RFC 6238): the most common app-based form. Server and app share a secret seed; both compute a 6-8 digit code based on current time (usually a 30-second window) and HMAC-SHA1. Works offline. Codes rotate every 30s.

HOTP (HMAC-based OTP, RFC 4226): counter-based instead of time-based. Each press of a hardware token increments a counter. Server and token must stay in sync. Less common than TOTP.

Weaknesses: SMS OTP is vulnerable to SIM swap attacks and SS7 interception. Push notifications are vulnerable to MFA fatigue (spam the user with approvals until they click yes). FIDO2 hardware keys are the strongest form of Have-factor.

hack.memory: Have = something physical. Phone with TOTP app, YubiKey, smart card. TOTP = 30s time-based code (RFC 6238). HOTP = counter-based (RFC 4226). SMS is weakest because SIM swap attacks bypass it.
hack.practice: Set up Google Authenticator on any account (Gmail, GitHub). Note the 30-second rotation. Then scan the same QR into a second device — both apps show the same code because they share the seed. This demonstrates how TOTP works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Know TOTP (time, RFC 6238) vs HOTP (counter, RFC 4226). Know that SMS is still Have-factor but the weakest form. FIDO2 hardware keys are gold standard.

5.4.c.info:
The 'Something you ARE' factor is a measurable physical or behavioral characteristic of the user — a biometric. Because it is inherent to the individual, it cannot be forgotten or transferred like a password, and it's much harder to replicate than stealing a physical token.

Common biometric types:

  • Fingerprint — most deployed; capacitive or optical sensors on phones, laptops, door locks. Fast, familiar, moderately hard to spoof.
  • Facial recognition — Apple Face ID (IR + depth map), Windows Hello. Strong when done with depth sensing; weaker with 2D cameras (photo spoofing).
  • Iris scan — patterns of the colored ring; very accurate (~1 in 1.2M false match), used in high-security environments.
  • Retina scan — blood vessel pattern at the back of the eye; most accurate biometric but requires invasive scanner.
  • Voice recognition — vocal biometrics; convenient for phone auth but defeated by recordings.
  • Behavioral — keystroke cadence, gait, mouse movement. Continuous authentication.

Accuracy metrics:

  • FAR (False Accept Rate) — how often an impostor is wrongly accepted. Lower = more secure.
  • FRR (False Reject Rate) — how often a legitimate user is wrongly rejected. Lower = more usable.
  • EER / CER (Equal / Crossover Error Rate) — the threshold where FAR = FRR; lower EER = better system.

Fundamental weakness: biometrics cannot be revoked. If your fingerprint template is stolen from a database, you can't get new fingerprints. That is why biometrics should always be one factor of MFA — never the only factor.

hack.memory: Are = what's on your body. Fingerprint, face, iris. Cannot be changed if compromised — that's the killer weakness. FAR (bad false accept) vs FRR (annoying false reject), EER = the sweet spot.
hack.practice: Compare FaceID (IR depth) vs a laptop's 2D webcam facial login. Try to fool each with a photo of yourself — IR depth defeats it, 2D often does not. That's the gap between inherence factors that include liveness checks and ones that don't.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam may ask for biometric types (fingerprint, iris, face, voice, retina), or for the metric names (FAR, FRR, EER/CER). Also know the 'cannot be changed' weakness.

5.4.c.info:
The most common MFA misunderstanding — and the most frequently tested CCNA security question — is that two credentials from the SAME category do not constitute MFA. The independence of the factors is the whole point. If both can be stolen by the same attack vector, you haven't added security, you've added annoyance.

Classic NOT-MFA combinations:

  • Password + security question — both 'Something you Know.' A phishing page can harvest both in one form.
  • Password + PIN — both 'Know.' Same attack vector.
  • Two passwords — obviously both 'Know.'
  • Fingerprint + face scan — both 'Are.' A full biometric-database breach compromises both.
  • Smart card + YubiKey — both 'Have.' A thief who grabs your bag gets both.

Correct MFA combinations:

  • Password + phone TOTP (Know + Have)
  • Password + fingerprint (Know + Are)
  • Smart card + PIN (Have + Know)
  • Badge tap + iris scan (Have + Are)

The test: ask yourself — could an attacker steal both factors using the same technique? If yes, the factors are in the same category and it's not MFA. If no, the factors are independent and it is MFA.

Exam framing: questions will list pairs and ask 'which is MFA?' Others will describe a scenario ('a user logs in with a password and answers a security question') and ask if it's MFA (answer: no). Be ready to classify any combination on sight.

hack.memory: Same-category = NOT MFA. The attack vector test: if one phishing email can steal both, they're the same factor. Two Know = fail. Two Have = fail. Two Are = fail. MUST mix categories.
hack.practice: Rapid-fire drill: Password + pattern lock (both Know, NOT MFA). Password + Face ID (Know + Are, MFA). Smart card + YubiKey (both Have, NOT MFA). Password + SMS (Know + Have, MFA). 10 reps builds exam-speed classification.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. This is the #1 MFA trap question on the CCNA. Always answer: factors must be in DIFFERENT categories. Security questions are Know-factor, not a bonus second factor.

5.4.d.info:
The entire security value of MFA comes from the independence of the factors. Each factor category has a distinct compromise mechanism: knowledge is phished, possession is stolen, inherence is biometrically captured. When you require two categories, the attacker must run two different attacks against two different systems.

Independence in practice:

  • A phishing email can steal any Know factor instantly — password, PIN, security question answer, passphrase. It cannot steal a Have factor (the YubiKey is on the user's keyring).
  • A lost or stolen laptop might give the attacker a cached password AND a smart card left in the reader (Know + Have). They still can't replicate the user's fingerprint (Are).
  • A biometric template breach compromises the Are factor. The user's password and hardware token are untouched.

Compound attack vs independent factors: suppose an attacker breaches your Gmail. With one factor (password), game over. With true MFA (password + phone TOTP), they still need your phone. With adversary-in-the-middle phishing kits (Evilginx, Modlishka), even TOTP codes can be relayed in real time — which is why FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys (domain-bound, phishing-resistant) are the current gold standard second factor.

Design principle: when picking MFA factors, ask 'what single compromise defeats this?' If the answer is any single event, you don't have MFA — you have two-step verification at best. If the answer requires the attacker to simultaneously achieve two unrelated compromises, you have real MFA.

Layered defense: MFA is not a silver bullet. Pair it with rate limiting, anomaly detection (impossible travel, new device alerts), and session-level checks. MFA blocks most credential-stuffing and basic phishing; layered controls catch the advanced attackers who can still defeat SMS or push-based MFA.

hack.memory: Independence = the point of MFA. Each category needs a DIFFERENT attack to defeat. Phishing grabs Know, theft grabs Have, biometric breach grabs Are. Two factors, same category = one attack defeats both.
hack.practice: For each of your critical accounts (email, bank, cloud), list the exact MFA factors enabled. Then for each pair, write down the single attack that would defeat it. If you can't think of ONE attack that defeats both, you have real MFA. If you can, upgrade.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam framing: 'why is MFA more secure than 2 passwords?' = because the factors are independent / come from different categories. Memorize the word 'independent.'

5.4.d.info:
Below are the combinations most likely to appear on the CCNA exam. Memorize each, classify each, and be able to explain why it does or does not qualify as MFA.

TRUE MFA examples:

  • Password + SMS OTP — Know + Have. Weak Have (SIM swap risk) but still MFA by category.
  • Password + authenticator app TOTP — Know + Have. Strong, offline, phishing-resistant if codes aren't relayed.
  • Password + FIDO2 key — Know + Have. Strongest consumer MFA today; domain-bound, phishing-proof.
  • Password + fingerprint — Know + Are. Common on phones (unlock PIN + biometric).
  • Smart card + PIN — Have + Know. Standard for US federal PIV; CAC cards use this model.
  • Badge + iris scan — Have + Are. High-security data centers.
  • Password + push approval — Know + Have (push on a trusted device). Watch for MFA fatigue attacks.

NOT MFA (same category — classic traps):

  • Password + security question — both Know. Still one of the most common deployments and always wrong on the exam.
  • Password + PIN — both Know.
  • Two passwords / two PINs — both Know.
  • Fingerprint + face scan — both Are.
  • Smart card + YubiKey — both Have.

2FA vs MFA: Two-Factor Authentication is the specific case of MFA with exactly two factors. All 2FA is MFA, but MFA may use three or more factors in very high-security environments (password + token + biometric). The terms are often used interchangeably in marketing; the exam treats them the same as long as the categories differ.

hack.memory: Memorize the lists. Valid: Password + TOTP. Password + fingerprint. Smart card + PIN. Badge + iris. Not valid: password + anything-else-you-know. Two of the same category. Three categories = still MFA.
hack.practice: Make a 20-item quiz of random auth pairs. Classify each in under 3 seconds: MFA or NOT MFA. Shuffle and redo until your answers are automatic. Exam-day speed comes from drilling this pattern.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam testers love tricky pairs like 'password + YubiKey PIN' (Know + something-you-enter-on-Have — still arguably K+H because the YubiKey is Have). Keep it simple: categorize each element, check if different categories.

5.4.e.info:
A digital certificate is a cryptographically signed document that binds a public key to an identity (a person, server, device, or organization). Certificates are the foundation of secure identity on the internet — they underpin HTTPS, IPsec, 802.1X, SSH key auth, code signing, and S/MIME email.

Why certificates exist: public-key cryptography lets anyone encrypt data with your public key that only your private key can decrypt — BUT there's a distribution problem. How do I know this public key really belongs to www.bank.com and not an attacker? A certificate solves this by having a trusted third party (a Certificate Authority) digitally sign the public key together with the identity. If you trust the CA, you can trust the binding.

X.509 is the standard format. Defined by ITU-T. Version 3 (X.509v3) is current. Files come in several encodings:

  • PEM (.pem, .crt, .cer) — Base64-encoded, bounded by -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- / -----END CERTIFICATE-----. Human-readable, most common on Linux/Unix.
  • DER (.der, .cer) — binary encoding. Used on Windows and some hardware.
  • PKCS#12 (.pfx, .p12) — bundle of certificate + private key + chain, password-protected. Used for import/export.

Where you see certificates on CCNA equipment: HTTPS device management (self-signed by default), IPsec peer auth (cert-based vs pre-shared key), 802.1X EAP-TLS supplicants, SSH user certs in advanced deployments. Most CCNA questions focus on PKI concepts rather than command-line configuration.

hack.memory: Cert = public key + identity + CA signature. X.509 = the standard format. PEM (text) vs DER (binary) vs PKCS#12 (bundle with private key). Solves: 'how do I trust a public key?'
hack.practice: In Chrome, click the padlock on any HTTPS site → 'Connection is secure' → 'Certificate is valid.' Read every field: Subject, Issuer, Validity, Public key, SAN, serial. Export it as PEM and inspect with `openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout`.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam expects conceptual knowledge — what a cert is, what format, who signs it, what it binds. Deep crypto is out of scope.

5.4.e.term: Certificate Authority (CA) and chain of trust
5.4.e.info:
A Certificate Authority (CA) is a trusted third party that issues and signs certificates. CAs are the foundation of the PKI trust model — without a trustworthy CA, certificates mean nothing.

The trust chain:

  • Root CA — self-signed, pre-installed in operating systems and browsers (the 'trust store'). Root CAs are the anchors; trust them and you trust everyone they vouch for. Examples: DigiCert Global Root, ISRG Root X1 (Let's Encrypt), GlobalSign Root CA.
  • Intermediate CA — signed by the root (or by another intermediate). Used to do the actual cert issuance. Intermediates exist so the root's private key can stay offline in a vault — compromise of an intermediate is recoverable (revoke it), compromise of a root is catastrophic.
  • End-entity certificate — signed by an intermediate. The 'leaf' cert on a web server, VPN peer, or client device.

Validation algorithm: when your browser sees a certificate, it walks the chain: check the leaf's signature with the intermediate's public key, check the intermediate's signature with the root's public key, and stop when it hits a root it already trusts. If the chain breaks (missing intermediate, expired cert, untrusted root), the browser shows a warning.

Revocation:

  • CRL (Certificate Revocation List) — periodically published list of revoked cert serial numbers. Clients download and cache. Large, slow, and often skipped.
  • OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) — real-time query; client asks the CA 'is this cert still valid?' by serial number. Faster than CRL, but privacy-leaking (CA sees which sites you visit) and dependent on CA availability.
  • OCSP stapling — server pre-fetches a signed 'still valid' response from the CA and hands it to clients during TLS handshake. Solves OCSP's privacy and latency problems.

Public CAs (DigiCert, Sectigo, Let's Encrypt) issue certs valid globally. Private/internal CAs (Microsoft AD Certificate Services, Cisco ISE as a CA) issue certs trusted only by your organization — common for IPsec peer certs, 802.1X, and internal HTTPS.

hack.memory: CA = trust anchor. Chain: Root → Intermediate → End-entity. Browser trusts root → trusts intermediate → trusts leaf. Revocation: CRL (list) or OCSP (real-time). Private CA = internal PKI.
hack.practice: Run `openssl s_client -connect github.com:443 -showcerts` and read the chain that prints. Identify the leaf (subject: github.com), the intermediate (Sectigo / DigiCert), and the root. Verify each signature walks up to a trusted anchor.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests the chain-of-trust concept and the CRL/OCSP distinction. Know: root is self-signed, intermediate signs leaves, both revocation methods exist.

5.4.e.info:
An X.509 certificate is a structured document with required and optional fields. Memorizing the core fields is exam-relevant and helps you read real certificates fluently.

Core fields:

  • Version — almost always v3.
  • Serial number — unique per-CA identifier for this cert. Used by CRL/OCSP for revocation lookups.
  • Signature algorithm — how the CA signed the cert (e.g., sha256WithRSAEncryption, ecdsa-with-SHA384).
  • Issuer — distinguished name of the CA that signed the cert.
  • Validity — Not Before and Not After timestamps. Certs expire.
  • Subject — distinguished name of the entity the cert identifies (CN=www.cisco.com, O=Cisco Systems, C=US).
  • Subject Public Key Info — the public key itself plus its algorithm (RSA 2048, ECDSA P-256, etc.).
  • Extensions (v3) — including Subject Alternative Names (SAN), Extended Key Usage (serverAuth, clientAuth, codeSigning), Basic Constraints (is this a CA?), Authority Key Identifier, CRL Distribution Points, Authority Information Access (OCSP URL).
  • Signature — the CA's actual cryptographic signature over everything above.

SAN (Subject Alternative Name) is now the primary identity field — modern browsers ignore CN and only check SAN. A single cert can cover many DNS names and IPs via SAN entries.

Inspecting a cert:

openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout

Read every field. The exam may show a cert-content screenshot and ask you to identify the issuer, subject, validity, or public key — being fluent with this output saves time.

hack.memory: X.509 fields: Subject (who), Issuer (CA), Public key, Validity (not-before/not-after), Serial, Signature algorithm, SAN (modern identity), Extensions (EKU, CRL, OCSP URLs). Modern browsers check SAN, not CN.
hack.practice: openssl x509 -in /path/to/cert.pem -text -noout. Read every field aloud. Find the SAN list, the EKU (serverAuth?), the CRL Distribution Points URL, the OCSP responder URL. This builds real cert fluency.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam might list 4-5 X.509 fields and ask which is missing. Memorize: Subject, Issuer, Validity, Serial, Public key, Signature. SAN is an extension, often highlighted.

5.4.e.info:
PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is the complete framework of policies, procedures, hardware, software, and people that manage digital certificates through their lifecycle: issuance, distribution, storage, renewal, and revocation.

PKI components:

  • CA (Certificate Authority) — issues and signs certs. Root and intermediate CAs.
  • RA (Registration Authority) — validates the identity of requesters before the CA issues. In small PKIs, the CA and RA are the same entity.
  • Certificate repository — where certs and CRLs are published for clients to fetch.
  • Certificate lifecycle management — request (CSR), issuance, distribution, use, renewal, revocation, archival.
  • End entities — users, devices, servers that hold certs.

Certificate Signing Request (CSR): the standard way to request a cert. The requester generates a keypair, places the public key + identity info into a CSR, signs it with the private key (proving possession), and sends the CSR to the CA. The CA validates, signs, and returns the finished cert. The private key never leaves the requester.

openssl req -new -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout key.pem -out csr.pem

Internal / private PKI on Cisco:

  • Cisco IOS PKI — routers/switches can act as a small CA using crypto pki server.
  • Cisco ISE — full-featured enterprise CA for 802.1X, BYOD, VPN.
  • Microsoft ADCS — Active Directory Certificate Services, common in Windows shops.

CCNA exposure: you won't configure a full PKI on the exam, but you need to know the roles (CA, RA, end entity), the CSR flow, and the lifecycle steps. Know that certs expire and must be renewed before Not After, or TLS/IPsec/etc. will break.

hack.memory: PKI = the whole ecosystem. CA issues, RA validates, repository distributes, CRL/OCSP handles revocation. CSR flow: generate keypair, send public key + identity, receive signed cert. Private key never leaves the requester.
hack.practice: Generate a CSR: `openssl req -new -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout priv.key -out req.csr -subj '/CN=test.local'`. Inspect with `openssl req -in req.csr -text -noout`. Submit to your internal CA (or self-sign for practice) and install.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Know the PKI roles (CA, RA, end entity) and the CSR workflow. Exam won't require full command syntax, but terminology fluency is essential.

5.4.f.info:
Biometrics are measurable physical or behavioral characteristics used to authenticate identity. Each type has a specific accuracy profile, ease of use, and threat model. For the CCNA, recognize the major types and their trade-offs.

Physical biometrics:

  • Fingerprint — most widely deployed. Capacitive sensors measure electrical differences across ridges; optical scan images the print. Modern sensors include liveness detection to defeat latex molds. Weakness: fingerprints can be lifted from surfaces the user touches.
  • Facial recognition — 2D camera + CNN-based matching is convenient but spoofable with photos or masks. Apple FaceID uses a structured infrared dot projector (~30,000 dots) to build a 3D depth map, defeating most spoof attempts. Windows Hello uses similar IR + camera pairs.
  • Iris scan — analyzes the unique texture of the colored ring around the pupil. Extremely accurate (~1 in 1.2M false match). Requires specialized near-infrared camera. Used in government border control (UAE, India Aadhaar).
  • Retina scan — maps the blood vessel pattern at the back of the eye using a low-power beam. Most accurate biometric by raw numbers but intrusive; never caught on commercially.
  • Voice recognition — speaker verification based on vocal tract and speech patterns. Convenient for phone IVR. Weak to recordings and deepfake audio.
  • Vein pattern (palm / finger) — near-infrared imaging of subcutaneous veins. Hard to spoof. Used in Japanese banking ATMs.

Behavioral biometrics:

  • Keystroke dynamics — cadence and pressure patterns while typing.
  • Gait — how you walk; measurable via camera or phone accelerometer.
  • Mouse movement — micro-patterns of cursor motion.
  • Signature dynamics — pressure, speed, acceleration during signing (not just the shape).

Behavioral biometrics are usually continuous — they run in the background after initial login and flag anomalies mid-session. They are not standalone primary factors but excellent for risk scoring.

hack.memory: Physical = body measurements (fingerprint, face, iris, retina, voice, vein). Behavioral = how you behave (typing, gait, mouse). Physical = primary factor. Behavioral = continuous/risk-scoring.
hack.practice: Test spoofing resistance: try to unlock a laptop's 2D webcam login with a printed photo of yourself. Try the same on FaceID (IR depth). The difference is liveness detection. This drives home why '3D / IR' biometrics are stronger than 2D.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam will list biometric types and ask you to identify them or classify as 'Are.' Know the physical types by name: fingerprint, face, iris, retina, voice.

5.4.f.info:
The fundamental weakness of biometric authentication is that biometric data cannot be changed if compromised. Unlike a password (rotatable) or a hardware token (replaceable), your fingerprints, iris pattern, and facial geometry are fixed for life. If a database of biometric templates is breached, the users affected have no recourse.

Real-world examples:

  • OPM breach (2015) — the U.S. Office of Personnel Management breach exposed 5.6 million fingerprint records from federal background check files. Those fingerprints can never be reset.
  • Biostar 2 (2019) — a biometric access control system leaked unencrypted fingerprint and facial recognition data for over 1M users.
  • Aadhaar (ongoing) — India's national biometric ID has seen multiple leaks of demographic + biometric data.

Mitigations (partial):

  • Template protection — store a one-way hash / feature vector, not the raw biometric image. Even this is imperfect because researchers have shown fingerprint reconstruction from templates.
  • Cancelable biometrics — apply a secret transform to the biometric before storing; if compromised, reissue with a new transform. Niche technology.
  • Local-only storage — Apple's Secure Enclave, Android's TEE, Windows Hello's TPM keep templates on-device and never upload to servers. This dramatically reduces breach surface.
  • MFA pairing — always combine biometrics with another factor so a stolen template alone is not enough.

Exam framing: 'What is the main disadvantage of biometrics compared to passwords?' → cannot be changed if compromised. 'Why should biometrics not be the sole authentication factor?' → because if breached, they can't be re-issued.

hack.memory: Biometrics cannot be revoked. Once your fingerprint template is stolen, it's stolen forever — you cannot reissue fingerprints. Mitigation: local-only storage (Secure Enclave, TPM), always pair with another factor.
hack.practice: Search 'Biostar 2 breach' and 'OPM fingerprint breach' — read what was leaked. Then check whether your biometrics are stored on-device only (Apple/Android/Windows Hello) or uploaded to a cloud service. On-device is a critical distinction.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam frames it as: 'which cannot be reset if compromised?' = biometric. Also 'why not use biometrics alone?' = non-revocable.

5.4.f.term: FAR, FRR, EER — biometric accuracy metrics
5.4.f.info:
Biometric systems do not match exactly — they compare a live sample to a stored template and score the similarity. A threshold decides whether the score is close enough to call it a match. Two error types emerge from this threshold-based matching.

FAR (False Acceptance Rate / False Match Rate): how often the system wrongly accepts an impostor. A false accept is a security failure — someone who isn't you got in. Lowering the matching threshold (being stricter) reduces FAR.

FRR (False Rejection Rate / False Non-Match Rate): how often the system wrongly rejects a legitimate user. A false reject is a usability failure — you couldn't log in to your own account. Raising the threshold (being looser) reduces FRR.

The trade-off: FAR and FRR move in opposite directions as you change the threshold. Too strict → low FAR, high FRR (secure but frustrating). Too loose → low FRR, high FAR (easy but insecure). Every biometric system has to pick a point on this curve.

EER / CER (Equal Error Rate / Crossover Error Rate): the threshold setting where FAR = FRR. The EER is a single number describing overall system accuracy — lower EER means the biometric is better across the board. Iris scanners have EERs near 0.0001%; consumer fingerprint scanners might be 0.01-0.1%.

Tuning advice:

  • High-security environments (government, banking) choose thresholds with very low FAR, accepting more false rejects.
  • Consumer convenience deployments (phone unlock) lean toward low FRR, accepting higher FAR.
  • When comparing biometric products, compare EER and the FAR/FRR curve shape, not just the threshold at default.

Exam framing: know which is 'bad for security' (FAR) vs 'bad for usability' (FRR), and that EER/CER is the crossover point used to rank systems.

hack.memory: FAR = False Accept (BAD for security). FRR = False Reject (annoying for users). EER/CER = point where FAR=FRR (use to rank systems). Lower EER = better biometric.
hack.practice: Graph FAR and FRR against threshold on paper. Watch them cross — that's EER. Picture what happens at each extreme: threshold=0 (everything accepted, FAR=100%), threshold=max (nothing accepted, FRR=100%). The system lives in the middle.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam may ask which metric is a 'security problem' (FAR) or 'usability problem' (FRR). EER lets you compare products.

5.5.a.info:
A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates a secure, encrypted tunnel across an untrusted network — typically the public internet — so that two endpoints can exchange traffic as if they were on a private network. VPNs provide confidentiality, integrity, and authentication for traffic that would otherwise be readable by anyone on the path.

Core VPN ideas:

  • Tunneling — original packets are encapsulated inside new packets addressed to the VPN peer. The inner packet (plus any private addressing) is hidden from the underlying network.
  • Encryption — the tunnel payload is encrypted (typically with AES) so eavesdroppers cannot read it.
  • Authentication — both ends verify each other's identity (via pre-shared key, certificate, or credentials) before the tunnel is established.
  • Integrity — each packet is hashed (SHA-256/384) so tampering in transit is detected.

VPN categories for CCNA:

  • Site-to-site — router-to-router, always on, connects whole networks. Usually IPsec.
  • Remote access — user device to VPN concentrator, on-demand, connects one user. IPsec or SSL/TLS (AnyConnect, OpenVPN, WireGuard).
  • Clientless SSL VPN — browser-based remote access via HTTPS, limited to web apps. No client install needed.
  • GRE over IPsec — for carrying multicast/routing protocols across an encrypted tunnel.
  • DMVPN — Cisco dynamic hub-and-spoke overlay using mGRE + NHRP + IPsec.

Why organizations use VPNs: connect branch offices cheaply (vs leased lines), let remote workers access internal resources securely, bypass geo-restrictions, extend a private network across a cloud provider. The dominant enterprise protocol is IPsec.

hack.memory: VPN = encrypted tunnel over untrusted network. Provides confidentiality + integrity + authentication. Flavors: site-to-site (router↔router), remote access (user↔VPN concentrator), clientless SSL (browser).
hack.practice: Map every VPN you've used: corporate laptop AnyConnect (remote access SSL VPN), home WireGuard to a VPS, branch-to-HQ IPsec on a Cisco ISR. For each, identify who the peers are and whether it's always-on or on-demand.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam covers VPN categories and IPsec specifics. Know the difference between site-to-site and remote access at a glance.

5.5.a.info:
A site-to-site VPN builds a persistent encrypted tunnel between two gateway devices (routers or firewalls) at different physical locations. All traffic between the two protected networks flows through the tunnel automatically. End users are completely unaware the VPN exists.

Characteristics:

  • Always on — the tunnel is established at boot (or on first interesting traffic) and stays up continuously, rekeying as lifetimes expire.
  • Router-to-router — encryption happens at the network edge on the VPN gateway. User devices send plain traffic to their default gateway; the gateway encrypts and tunnels it.
  • No client software — users never install anything. This makes site-to-site ideal for branch offices and partner connections.
  • Statically configured — both peers need matching IPsec parameters (IKE policy, transform set, peer IP, pre-shared key or cert, crypto ACL).

Interesting traffic: an ACL (crypto ACL) on each peer defines which traffic should be encrypted and sent through the tunnel. Usually this matches the two private subnets: traffic from 10.1.0.0/24 to 10.2.0.0/24 is interesting. Non-interesting traffic leaves the router normally (or is dropped by other policy).

Common configurations on IOS:

  • Crypto map — older method. Apply a crypto map to an outgoing interface; the map combines peer, interesting-traffic ACL, and transform set.
  • Static VTI (Virtual Tunnel Interface) — modern method. A tunnel interface carries the encrypted traffic; routing decides what enters. Simpler and more flexible.

Use cases: branch-to-HQ, multi-site networks, connecting on-prem data center to cloud VPC, partner extranets. Dominates enterprise WAN designs over the public internet.

hack.memory: Site-to-site = always-on router↔router tunnel. Users don't know it exists. Configured statically with matching params on both ends. Interesting traffic ACL defines what goes through the tunnel.
hack.practice: Diagram it: Branch LAN 10.1.0.0/24 → Branch router Gi0/0 → public internet (encrypted) → HQ router Gi0/0 → HQ LAN 10.2.0.0/24. Label the interesting-traffic ACL: `permit ip 10.1.0.0 0.0.0.255 10.2.0.0 0.0.0.255`.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'two offices need permanent connectivity' = site-to-site. 'No client software required' = site-to-site. 'Router-to-router' = site-to-site.

5.5.a.info:
A remote access VPN allows an individual user device (laptop, phone) to securely connect to the corporate network from any location. Unlike site-to-site, remote access is on-demand — the user or OS initiates the connection when corporate resources are needed.

Characteristics:

  • On-demand — user clicks connect, authenticates, the tunnel comes up, and disconnects when done (or after idle timeout).
  • Client-to-site — encryption terminates at the user device on one end and a VPN concentrator / firewall at the corporate site on the other.
  • Client software required — Cisco AnyConnect / Secure Client, GlobalProtect, OpenVPN client, or built-in OS VPN client (IKEv2 in macOS/Windows, strongSwan on Linux).
  • Per-user authentication — every user logs in with their own credentials (password + MFA, or certificates). Per-user accountability and logging.

Implementation types:

  • IPsec remote access — traditional; requires the IPsec client. Often IKEv2 now.
  • SSL/TLS VPN (AnyConnect) — the dominant modern form. Uses TCP 443, traverses most firewalls, works anywhere HTTPS works. Full tunnel or split tunnel configurable.
  • Clientless SSL VPN — browser portal (HTTPS). No client installed. Limited to web apps and a few other proxied protocols. Good for unmanaged devices.
  • WireGuard / Tailscale — newer UDP-based overlay VPN, fast and simple. Increasingly common outside classic enterprise.

Split tunnel vs full tunnel: full tunnel sends all device traffic through the corporate VPN (good for consistent policy, bad for user bandwidth). Split tunnel sends only corporate-destined traffic through the VPN; everything else goes direct (faster, but external traffic bypasses corporate security).

Typical use cases: employees on the road, work from home, BYOD, emergency admin access.

hack.memory: Remote access = on-demand client-to-site VPN. User initiates, authenticates, tunnel comes up. Client software required. Mostly SSL/TLS (AnyConnect) today. Per-user accountability.
hack.practice: Install Cisco AnyConnect or OpenVPN client against a lab VPN. Authenticate, connect, run `ipconfig` / `ifconfig` — note the new virtual interface + tunnel IP. Traceroute to an internal resource — hops go through the VPN gateway. Disconnect — traffic returns to local path.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam scenarios: 'traveling employee' → remote access. 'On-demand single user' → remote access. 'Clientless via browser' → SSL VPN subtype of remote access.

5.5.b.term: Scenario: traveling employee → remote access VPN
5.5.b.info:
When a single user needs to reach corporate resources from anywhere — airport WiFi, hotel, home — the correct answer is remote access VPN. The user is one endpoint of the tunnel; the corporate VPN concentrator is the other.

Why it must be remote access:

  • The user's device IP is dynamic and unpredictable (whatever the current WiFi hands them). A site-to-site tunnel requires both peer IPs to be known and stable.
  • The user should be authenticated personally (per-user credentials + MFA) for accountability. Site-to-site peers authenticate as devices, not users.
  • The tunnel is only needed while the user is working remotely; always-on for one laptop makes no sense.

Typical deployment — Cisco AnyConnect (Secure Client):

  • VPN concentrator (ASA, Firepower FTD, Meraki MX, or ISR with SSL VPN module).
  • AnyConnect client on the user's laptop/phone.
  • Authentication: AD credentials + MFA (Duo push is very common with AnyConnect).
  • Optionally: posture/compliance check before allowing the tunnel (up-to-date AV, disk encryption, patch level).
  • Split tunnel (default route NOT pushed) or full tunnel (default route pushed) per policy.

Typical user flow: open AnyConnect → type HQ VPN URL → enter username/password → approve MFA push → tunnel up → laptop gets an internal IP → internal DNS flips → user works as if at their desk → disconnect when done.

Exam cue phrases: 'traveling user,' 'work from home,' 'mobile worker,' 'individual user,' 'dynamic source IP,' 'on-demand.' All point to remote access VPN.

hack.memory: Traveling employee = remote access VPN (AnyConnect). Dynamic source IP, personal credentials + MFA, on-demand. Exam cue words: 'single user,' 'mobile,' 'from anywhere.'
hack.practice: Configure AnyConnect in a Cisco CML/ASA lab. Connect from a second host through NAT. Observe: dynamic client IP, tunnel up only while connected, per-user log entries on the concentrator. Check split vs full tunnel behavior with a traceroute.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Which VPN type for a traveling employee?' → Remote access. 'Client-to-site, on-demand' → Remote access.

5.5.b.info:
When two networks (not users) need to communicate privately and continuously over the internet, the correct answer is site-to-site VPN. The tunnel lives on the routers; every user on both sides gets transparent access without any client software.

Why it must be site-to-site:

  • Many users on both ends — deploying client software to every user is operationally absurd.
  • Traffic should always be available — the tunnel must be up 24/7, not dependent on a user clicking 'connect.'
  • Peers have static (or predictable) public IPs. Static IPs make always-on IPsec reliable.
  • Device-level authentication (pre-shared key or certificate) is sufficient; per-user auth happens at application layer.

Typical design:

  • Branch office: Cisco ISR (or Meraki MX) with a public IP. Crypto map or VTI-based IPsec tunnel to HQ. Interesting traffic = 'branch subnet to HQ subnet.'
  • HQ: ASA or ISR with public IP. Matching IPsec config. Dynamic routing over the tunnel (if using VTI + GRE/IPsec or DMVPN).
  • Users on the branch LAN send packets to their default gateway (the branch ISR). The ISR encrypts and tunnels them to the HQ router. HQ decrypts and delivers to the destination.

Variations:

  • Hub-and-spoke — one HQ plus many branches, each with a tunnel to HQ. Traffic between branches traverses HQ.
  • Full mesh — every branch to every other branch. Scales poorly (N²).
  • DMVPN — hub-and-spoke with on-demand spoke-to-spoke tunnels. Best of both worlds.
  • SD-WAN — modern overlay that builds and manages tunnels automatically across multiple underlays (MPLS, broadband, LTE).

Exam cue phrases: 'connect two offices,' 'branch to headquarters,' 'always on,' 'users unaware of VPN,' 'no client software.' All point to site-to-site.

hack.memory: Branch-to-HQ = site-to-site VPN. Always on, router-to-router, no client software. Static IPs, device auth (PSK/cert). Hub-and-spoke is typical. DMVPN/SD-WAN are scaled-up variants.
hack.practice: Draw a 3-branch hub-and-spoke on paper. Each branch has a public IP. Each has a tunnel to HQ. Label the interesting-traffic ACL for each tunnel. Now extend the drawing to include DMVPN — spokes build on-demand tunnels to each other when needed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'two offices, always on, users unaware' → site-to-site. 'No client software' → site-to-site.

5.5.b.info:
Clientless SSL VPN is a remote access VPN subtype that runs entirely in the user's web browser over HTTPS. There is no VPN client software to install — the user visits a URL, authenticates, and gets a web portal with links to corporate resources.

How it works:

  • User browses to the VPN URL (https://vpn.company.com).
  • Browser establishes TLS to the VPN concentrator.
  • User authenticates (AD, MFA, cert).
  • Concentrator presents a portal with links / bookmarks to internal web apps, file shares, RDP sessions, etc.
  • Clicks on those links are proxied through the concentrator over HTTPS.

Capabilities and limits:

  • Great for — intranet web apps (SharePoint, internal dashboards), email via Outlook Web Access, anything accessible via HTTP/HTTPS. Also RDP-over-HTML5 via Java/HTML5 clients on the portal.
  • Not for — full network access, arbitrary TCP/UDP protocols, applications that don't proxy cleanly over HTTP. For those, you need a full-tunnel client (AnyConnect).
  • Good for unmanaged or partner devices where you can't install a VPN client.

Deployment on Cisco: Cisco ASA and Firepower Threat Defense both support clientless SSL VPN. It has been gradually deprecated in favor of the fuller AnyConnect experience, but you will still see it on CCNA material.

Security note: clientless access is still a remote access VPN and still needs strong authentication (MFA). Because there's no client, device posture is hard to verify — always pair with MFA and a short idle timeout.

hack.memory: Clientless SSL VPN = remote access via browser HTTPS. No client to install. Portal proxies web apps. Great for unmanaged devices / partners. Not a full network VPN — web apps only.
hack.practice: On a Cisco ASA or FTD lab, configure the clientless WebVPN feature. Add a bookmark for an internal site. Connect via browser — authenticate — click the bookmark and watch the URL rewrite at the ASA. Compare the experience to AnyConnect tunneled access.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam cue: 'browser-only, no client, access web apps' → clientless SSL VPN. Know it's a subtype of remote access.

5.5.c.info:
Confidentiality is the IPsec security service that ensures data cannot be read by unauthorized parties during transit. It is delivered by encryption: the sender encrypts the payload with a shared key, and only holders of the matching key can decrypt.

Symmetric encryption algorithms used in IPsec:

  • AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) — current best practice. 128, 192, or 256-bit keys. Block cipher. AES-GCM combines encryption and integrity in one operation (authenticated encryption) and is preferred in modern deployments.
  • 3DES (Triple DES) — legacy, deprecated. 168-bit effective key. Still seen in old configurations; avoid for new work.
  • DES (Data Encryption Standard) — obsolete. 56-bit key. Trivially brute-forced today. Never use.

How keys get there: the actual encryption keys are short-lived and are negotiated dynamically by IKE (Internet Key Exchange). Peers perform a Diffie-Hellman exchange to derive a shared secret over an insecure channel without ever transmitting the key itself. The resulting key material is used to encrypt IPsec traffic.

Where encryption happens: only ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) provides encryption in IPsec. AH (Authentication Header) does not encrypt — it only authenticates. Modern deployments use ESP almost exclusively.

Tunnel mode vs transport mode: in tunnel mode, ESP encrypts the entire original packet (including the original IP header). In transport mode, ESP encrypts only the payload, leaving the original IP header visible. Site-to-site VPNs use tunnel mode by default.

Exam framing: 'Which IPsec service prevents eavesdroppers from reading data?' → Confidentiality. 'Which algorithm provides confidentiality?' → AES (or 3DES/DES legacy).

hack.memory: Confidentiality = can't READ the data. Provided by encryption (AES, 3DES legacy, DES broken). ESP encrypts; AH does NOT. Keys come from Diffie-Hellman in IKE.
hack.practice: On a lab IPsec tunnel, configure transform set `esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac`. Pass traffic. Capture on the WAN — payload is unreadable. Change to `esp-null` (no encryption, auth only) and re-capture — now you can read the payload. Stark contrast.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'which IPsec service = encryption' → confidentiality. Algorithms: AES (best), 3DES (legacy), DES (broken).

5.5.c.info:
Integrity is the IPsec service that ensures data has not been modified in transit. It is delivered by cryptographic hashing with a shared secret (HMAC): the sender computes a hash of the packet and attaches it; the receiver recomputes and compares. Any tampering changes the hash and is detected.

Hash algorithms used in IPsec:

  • HMAC-SHA-256 / 384 / 512 — current best practice. Strong against collisions and preimage attacks.
  • HMAC-SHA-1 — legacy. Still common in older configs; SHA-1 has known weaknesses but for HMAC usage remains reasonably robust. Phase out where possible.
  • HMAC-MD5 — deprecated. MD5 is broken for collision resistance; avoid.

HMAC vs plain hash: a plain hash (SHA-256 of a message) can be recomputed by anyone who sees the message — useless for integrity against an active attacker. HMAC mixes a shared secret into the hash computation; only parties with the secret can produce or verify the HMAC. This gives integrity + authentication of source.

Where it's used: both AH and ESP provide integrity. AH authenticates and integrity-checks the entire packet (including the IP header). ESP authenticates and integrity-checks the ESP header + encrypted payload (not the outer IP header). Both also cover replay protection via sequence numbers.

AEAD — the modern approach: Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data algorithms like AES-GCM combine encryption and integrity in one operation. This is faster and reduces misconfiguration risk vs separate cipher + HMAC choices. Modern IPsec implementations prefer AES-GCM.

Exam framing: 'Which IPsec service ensures data has not been modified?' → Integrity. 'Algorithm for integrity?' → SHA (SHA-256 modern, SHA-1 legacy, MD5 deprecated).

hack.memory: Integrity = data hasn't been CHANGED. Provided by HMAC hashing (SHA-256 modern, SHA-1 legacy, MD5 deprecated). Both AH and ESP give integrity. Modern pick: AES-GCM (AEAD = encrypt + integrity in one).
hack.practice: On the lab tunnel, set `esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac`. Capture a packet, flip one byte on the wire (Wireshark edit or use Scapy). Replay it — receiver drops it because the HMAC no longer matches. That's integrity in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'which IPsec service = data unchanged' → integrity. Algorithms: SHA family. MD5 obsolete.

5.5.c.info:
Authentication in IPsec proves that the peer on the other end of the tunnel is who they claim to be. Without peer authentication, any attacker who can intercept the IKE negotiation could impersonate a legitimate peer and establish a tunnel with you.

Two primary authentication methods:

  • Pre-Shared Key (PSK) — a secret string configured identically on both peers. Simple to set up but difficult to rotate at scale; a compromised PSK affects every tunnel using it. Good for small deployments, lab environments, and quick setups.
  • Digital certificates (PKI-based) — each peer has an X.509 certificate signed by a common CA. Peers exchange certs during IKE and validate each other's signatures. Scales far better than PSK and supports revocation. Standard for large enterprises and always-on deployments.

Where authentication happens: during IKE Phase 1. After Diffie-Hellman derives the session key, peers prove their identity using PSK or cert. If authentication fails, the Phase 1 ISAKMP SA never forms and no tunnel is built.

Extended Authentication (Xauth): some deployments layer user credentials on top of device-level authentication for remote access VPNs. The IPsec tunnel is authenticated with a cert or PSK at the device level, then the user is further prompted for username/password. Modern deployments typically do this via RADIUS/TACACS+ and MFA.

Exam framing: 'Which IPsec service confirms the peer's identity?' → Authentication. 'Authentication methods?' → PSK or digital certificates. 'Which scales better?' → Certificates.

hack.memory: Authentication = prove peer IDENTITY. Two methods: PSK (shared secret, simple) or certs (scales, revocable). Happens in IKE Phase 1. Fail auth = no tunnel.
hack.practice: Configure an IPsec tunnel twice — once with PSK (`crypto isakmp key Cisco123 address 1.1.1.1`) and once with certs (`crypto pki trustpoint ...`). Notice how PSK is trivial on 2 peers but would be a nightmare on 500. Certs feel heavier but scale cleanly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'which IPsec service = identity proof' → authentication. Know PSK vs cert tradeoff.

5.5.c.info:
Anti-replay protection prevents an attacker from capturing a legitimate IPsec packet and re-transmitting it later to trick the receiver into re-processing the traffic (a replay attack). Without anti-replay, an attacker could, for example, capture an encrypted 'transfer $1000' packet and replay it many times.

How it works:

  • Every IPsec packet carries a sequence number in the ESP (or AH) header, incrementing by 1 for each packet sent on that SA.
  • The receiver maintains a sliding window (default size 64, configurable). It tracks which sequence numbers have been seen recently.
  • Packets with sequence numbers outside the window (too old) or already seen in the window are dropped as replays.
  • When the counter approaches wrap-around (2^32-1 for the classic 32-bit sequence), a new SA is negotiated to reset the counter safely.

Enabling anti-replay: in IOS, anti-replay is on by default for IPsec. The window size can be configured with crypto ipsec security-association replay window-size [size]. Disabling anti-replay is rarely a good idea but sometimes needed for out-of-order QoS.

Both AH and ESP provide anti-replay — it's built into both protocols via the sequence number field. ESP adds encryption and is the dominant choice.

Interaction with QoS: aggressive QoS reordering can cause legitimate packets to arrive outside the window. If you see ESP replay drops in logs when QoS is in play, expand the window or re-examine the QoS policy. Modern IOS supports extended (64-bit) sequence numbers to mitigate both wrap and window issues.

Exam framing: 'Which IPsec service prevents captured packets from being re-sent?' → Anti-replay. 'What mechanism?' → Sequence numbers in ESP/AH header.

hack.memory: Anti-replay = can't REPLAY captured packets. Sequence numbers in ESP/AH header + sliding window at receiver. Default window 64. Counter wraps at 2^32, triggering SA rekey.
hack.practice: On a lab tunnel, capture a legitimate ESP packet. Use Scapy to resend it. Check the receiver's IPsec counters with `show crypto ipsec sa` — watch the replay drop counter increment. That's anti-replay in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'which IPsec service = captured packets can't be resent' → anti-replay. Implemented via sequence numbers.

5.5.d.term: AH (Authentication Header) — what it does and doesn't do
5.5.d.info:
AH (Authentication Header) is one of the two IPsec protocols defined by RFC 4302. It provides integrity, authentication, and anti-replay — but NO encryption. Data protected by AH alone is still readable by anyone who captures it; AH only proves the data is unmodified and from a verified sender.

What AH authenticates: AH protects the entire IP packet, including the outer IP header — except for mutable fields that legitimately change in transit (TTL, IP header checksum, DSCP bits in some cases). This 'whole-packet' authentication is AH's unique feature and also its undoing.

AH vs ESP quick compare:

  • AH — IP protocol 51. Integrity + authentication + anti-replay. No encryption. Authenticates IP header.
  • ESP — IP protocol 50. Integrity + authentication + anti-replay + encryption. Does NOT authenticate outer IP header.

Why AH is obsolete:

  • No encryption — defeats most practical VPN use cases.
  • Breaks through NAT — NAT modifies the IP header, which AH authenticates, so AH fails across NAT. Since NAT is everywhere, AH is essentially unusable on the internet.
  • ESP is a superset — ESP provides everything AH provides except outer-IP-header authentication, plus encryption, plus NAT-T.

In practice, modern IPsec deployments use ESP only. You'll rarely see AH in the wild — the CCNA tests it mainly so you can answer 'which protocol doesn't encrypt?' and 'which protocol breaks NAT?'

Packet structure (tunnel mode): new IP header → AH header → original IP header + payload. Everything after the new IP header is integrity-protected, nothing is encrypted.

hack.memory: AH = Authentication Header = integrity + auth, NO ENCRYPTION. IP proto 51. Authenticates the whole packet including IP header → breaks across NAT → obsolete in practice.
hack.practice: Draw an AH tunnel-mode packet side by side with an ESP tunnel-mode packet. Circle what's authenticated in each. Circle what's encrypted in each. AH = auth everything, encrypt nothing. ESP = auth everything except outer IP, encrypt the original packet.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'IPsec protocol with no encryption' → AH. 'Proto 51' → AH. 'Authenticates IP header' → AH. Also know AH is deprecated in practice.

5.5.d.info:
AH does not work across NAT — and this is the single biggest reason AH is rare in production. Understanding why requires looking at what NAT does and what AH authenticates.

NAT modifies the IP header — specifically the source IP (and often source port for PAT, destination IP for destination NAT). This is its whole purpose: translate addresses so traffic can traverse an address boundary.

AH authenticates the entire IP header. The sender computes an HMAC over the outer IP header + payload and stamps it into the AH header. The receiver recomputes the HMAC and compares.

The break: if a NAT device changes the source IP mid-flight, the receiver computes a different HMAC from the one the sender stamped. The mismatch looks like tampering, and the receiver drops the packet. There is no way to fix this without bypassing AH's whole reason for existing.

ESP is different: ESP authenticates the ESP header + payload but NOT the outer IP header. If NAT changes the outer IP, ESP's HMAC is unaffected. This is why ESP can coexist with NAT when combined with NAT Traversal (NAT-T).

NAT Traversal (NAT-T) for ESP: defined in RFC 3948. When IKE detects NAT on the path (during Phase 1), it automatically encapsulates ESP packets in UDP port 4500. The NAT device sees a normal UDP flow and translates it without breaking the embedded ESP. No equivalent exists for AH — there's no way to rescue AH across NAT.

Exam angle: 'Which IPsec protocol breaks across NAT?' → AH. 'Why?' → It authenticates the IP header, which NAT modifies. 'What solves this for ESP?' → NAT-T, encapsulating ESP in UDP 4500.

nodes: Sender stamps HMAC over IP hdr

hack.memory: AH + NAT = BROKEN. NAT changes IP header → AH HMAC no longer matches → drop. ESP is fine because it doesn't authenticate the outer IP header. ESP + NAT-T (UDP 4500) wraps ESP in UDP to cross NAT.
hack.practice: Build a lab: sender on one side of a NAT → ESP tunnel through NAT → works (with NAT-T, UDP 4500 visible in capture). Now try AH through the same NAT → fails immediately, drops visible in logs. This is the most memorable AH demonstration you can do.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'AH doesn't work with NAT because ___' → it authenticates the IP header that NAT modifies. Pair with ESP + NAT-T (UDP 4500) as the solution.

5.5.d.info:
Memorize the protocol number: AH uses IP protocol 51. This is the value placed in the IP header's Protocol field to signal 'the next header is AH.' Firewalls and ACLs must explicitly permit protocol 51 for AH traffic to pass.

Context — IP protocol numbers you should know for CCNA:

  • 1 — ICMP (ping, traceroute)
  • 2 — IGMP (multicast group membership)
  • 6 — TCP
  • 17 — UDP
  • 47 — GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation)
  • 50 — ESP
  • 51 — AH
  • 89 — OSPF
  • 88 — EIGRP
  • 115 — L2TP (often over UDP 1701 instead)

Why it matters:

  • ACLs / firewall rules — to permit AH traffic through a firewall, you must allow IP protocol 51 (not a TCP or UDP port — AH is its own IP-layer protocol).
  • Troubleshooting — if AH traffic is being dropped, check for protocol 51 permits on every device in the path.
  • Exam recognition — the exam loves to test the protocol numbers 50 (ESP) and 51 (AH) in isolation or side-by-side.

Mnemonic: 'ESP before AH alphabetically, so 50 before 51.' Or: 'ESP = Encrypted = the lower number (50) because it does more work.'

hack.memory: AH = IP protocol 51. ESP = IP protocol 50. ACL rules for IPsec must permit these proto numbers (not TCP/UDP ports). For ESP + NAT, also permit UDP 500 (IKE) and UDP 4500 (NAT-T).
hack.practice: Capture IPsec traffic in Wireshark. Filter `ip.proto == 50` to see ESP; filter `ip.proto == 51` if you can find any AH. Compare to UDP 500 and UDP 4500 for IKE/NAT-T. Locking in the numbers by seeing them live is hugely faster than rote memorization.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam guarantees 'ESP = 50, AH = 51.' Also know UDP 500 (IKE) and UDP 4500 (NAT-T). These 4 numbers are mandatory memorization.

5.5.e.info:
ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) is the dominant IPsec protocol, defined by RFC 4303. It provides all four IPsec security services: confidentiality (encryption), integrity, authentication, and anti-replay. Virtually every production IPsec VPN uses ESP, not AH.

What ESP does:

  • Encrypts everything after the ESP header — the encrypted region includes the original payload and, in tunnel mode, the original IP header.
  • Authenticates and integrity-checks the ESP header, the encrypted payload, and the ESP trailer — but NOT the outer IP header.
  • Anti-replay protects via sequence numbers in the ESP header and a sliding window at the receiver.

Packet structure (tunnel mode):

[ New IP header (proto 50) ] [ ESP header | SPI | SeqNum ] [ Orig IP + payload (ENCRYPTED) ] [ ESP trailer | padding | next hdr ] [ ESP auth (HMAC) ]

Cryptographic choices (transform set):

  • Encryption: AES-128 / AES-192 / AES-256 (CBC or GCM), 3DES (legacy), DES (obsolete).
  • Integrity: HMAC-SHA-256 / 384 / 512 (modern), HMAC-SHA-1 (legacy), HMAC-MD5 (deprecated).
  • AEAD: AES-GCM combines encryption and integrity in one operation — preferred choice.

IOS transform set syntax:

crypto ipsec transform-set STRONG esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac
 mode tunnel

ESP is NAT-compatible (with NAT-T wrapping in UDP 4500) because it doesn't authenticate the outer IP header. This is the main practical reason ESP won and AH lost.

Exam framing: 'Which IPsec protocol provides encryption?' → ESP. 'Protocol number?' → 50. 'Works across NAT?' → ESP (with NAT-T).

hack.memory: ESP = full protection: encryption + integrity + auth + anti-replay. IP proto 50. Modern transform: esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac or AES-GCM. NAT-compatible (via NAT-T UDP 4500).
hack.practice: Lab: `crypto ipsec transform-set MYSET esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac` → apply to crypto map → bring up tunnel → `show crypto ipsec sa` → verify transform and encrypted/decrypted packet counts. Capture on WAN — see IP proto 50.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'ESP provides ___' → encryption + integrity + auth + anti-replay. 'IP protocol ___' → 50. Memorize the transform syntax.

5.5.e.info:
ESP uses IP protocol 50, placed in the Protocol field of the outer IP header to signal 'next header is ESP.' Like AH's protocol 51, ESP is an IP-layer protocol — not a TCP/UDP port.

Firewall / ACL implications:

  • To let ESP traffic through a firewall, permit IP protocol 50 (not a port).
  • When NAT is in the path, you instead need to permit UDP port 4500 (NAT-T-wrapped ESP) and UDP port 500 (IKE negotiation).

The essential IPsec firewall list:

  • IP protocol 50 — ESP (raw, no NAT)
  • IP protocol 51 — AH (rare)
  • UDP 500 — IKE (Phase 1/2 negotiation)
  • UDP 4500 — NAT-T (ESP wrapped in UDP when NAT is present)

Verification on Cisco IOS:

show crypto ipsec sa
show crypto isakmp sa
show crypto session

These commands reveal active SPIs, transform sets, peer IPs, and counters. When the tunnel is up, you'll see MM_ACTIVE or QM_IDLE states for ISAKMP (Phase 1 up, Phase 2 idle between rekeys).

Exam framing: 'ESP uses IP protocol ___' → 50. 'ACL permits needed for IPsec VPN through a firewall?' → UDP 500, UDP 4500, IP 50 (and IP 51 if AH).

hack.memory: ESP = IP protocol 50. For firewall rules: permit IP proto 50 (ESP) + UDP 500 (IKE) + UDP 4500 (NAT-T). Four numbers total for IPsec: 50, 51, 500, 4500.
hack.practice: Build an IPsec tunnel through a firewall. Capture on WAN: see IP proto 50 (ESP) if no NAT, or UDP 4500 if NAT. Note UDP 500 (IKE) is present in both cases. Write a firewall ACL that permits exactly these 4 items and nothing else — works perfectly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Four numbers: 50, 51, 500, 4500. Guaranteed exam content.

5.5.e.info:
NAT Traversal (NAT-T) — RFC 3948 — is the mechanism that lets ESP (IP protocol 50) cross NAT devices that only understand TCP/UDP. Without NAT-T, ESP packets have no port number, and most NAT/PAT devices cannot keep state for them, so they fail. NAT-T wraps ESP inside a UDP header, giving NAT something to work with.

How NAT-T works:

  • During IKE Phase 1, peers exchange NAT-discovery payloads. Each peer hashes its own IP + port and compares to what the other peer sees. If the hashes don't match, NAT is in the path.
  • If NAT is detected, peers automatically switch to UDP 4500 and encapsulate ESP inside UDP from that point forward.
