  • IKE traffic also shifts from UDP 500 to UDP 4500 once NAT-T is active. Both IKE and ESP share port 4500.
  • NAT keepalives (small periodic UDP 4500 packets) keep the NAT translation from timing out. Default interval ~20 seconds on Cisco.

Before / after on the wire:

  • Without NAT: IP header (proto 50) → ESP header → encrypted payload.
  • With NAT-T: IP header (proto 17 = UDP) → UDP header (sport/dport 4500) → ESP header → encrypted payload.

Why AH has no equivalent: AH authenticates the outer IP header. Wrapping it in UDP doesn't fix that — the outer headers still get modified by NAT. AH has no NAT-T mechanism; it is fundamentally incompatible with NAT.

Verification:

show crypto ipsec sa   -- look for 'NAT traversal enabled' / UDP encapsulation

In Wireshark, filter udp.port == 4500 to see NAT-T traffic. The ESP payload is still encrypted and unreadable, but the UDP wrapper is visible.

Exam framing: 'How does ESP work across NAT?' → NAT-T encapsulates ESP in UDP 4500. 'Which port?' → 4500. 'Why does AH still break?' → AH authenticates the IP header and has no NAT-T equivalent.

hack.memory: NAT-T = wrap ESP inside UDP 4500 when NAT is detected. Auto-negotiated in IKE Phase 1. Both IKE and ESP share UDP 4500 after switchover. AH has no NAT-T — fundamentally broken across NAT.
hack.practice: Set up an IPsec tunnel with a NAT device in the middle. Start with NAT-T disabled (`no crypto ipsec nat-transparency udp-encapsulation`) → tunnel fails. Re-enable → tunnel comes up, UDP 4500 visible in capture. That's the before/after proof.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'What port does NAT-T use?' → UDP 4500. 'What protocol is wrapped?' → ESP. 'AH equivalent?' → None, AH can't do NAT-T.

5.5.e.term: ESP vs AH — the decision rule
5.5.e.info:
For every modern IPsec deployment, the decision is simple: use ESP. AH exists in the standards and appears on the CCNA, but its production use is essentially zero.

Direct head-to-head:

  • Encryption: ESP yes / AH no. This alone eliminates AH for confidentiality-sensitive traffic.
  • Integrity: both yes.
  • Authentication: both yes.
  • Anti-replay: both yes.
  • Authenticates outer IP header: AH yes / ESP no.
  • NAT-compatible: ESP yes (via NAT-T UDP 4500) / AH no.
  • IP protocol: ESP = 50 / AH = 51.

The theoretical niche for AH: environments where you need to authenticate the IP header itself (e.g., to detect IP spoofing of the outer header) and you have no NAT anywhere in the path. This combination essentially doesn't exist outside controlled research.

Combining AH + ESP: the standards allow nesting AH inside ESP or vice versa for maximum protection. In practice, nobody does this because it doubles overhead with negligible security benefit vs ESP's built-in integrity. The common pattern is ESP with integrity enabled — done.

Exam answer template:

  • 'Which IPsec protocol should I use in production?' → ESP.
  • 'Which supports encryption?' → ESP only.
  • 'Which works across NAT?' → ESP (with NAT-T).
  • 'Which is obsolete in practice?' → AH.
  • 'Which authenticates the outer IP header?' → AH.

hack.memory: Decision rule: ALWAYS USE ESP. AH exists for history + exam. ESP does everything AH does (except outer IP auth) PLUS encryption PLUS NAT support. There is no modern case for AH.
hack.practice: Make a two-column memory card: ESP vs AH. Check each cell: encryption (Y/N), integrity (Y/Y), auth (Y/Y), anti-replay (Y/Y), NAT (Y/N), proto (50/51). Say it out loud until it's automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: compare ESP vs AH. Almost every question points to ESP being the right choice.

5.5.f.info:
IKE (Internet Key Exchange) is the control-plane protocol for IPsec. It sets up the keys, algorithms, and authenticated peer relationships that ESP/AH need to protect data. IKE Phase 1 is the first half: it builds a secure management tunnel between the two peers so subsequent negotiations can happen safely.

What Phase 1 accomplishes:

  • Algorithm negotiation — peers exchange proposed cipher, hash, auth method, and DH group. Both pick the strongest mutually-supported set.
  • Diffie-Hellman key exchange — derive a shared secret over an insecure channel. Each peer sends a public DH value; each combines it with their own private value to compute the same shared key. No key material is transmitted.
  • Peer authentication — using PSK (shared secret) or certificates. Confirms you're talking to the right peer.
  • Output: ISAKMP SA (also called IKE SA) — a single bidirectional secure channel between the peers, used only for IKE traffic (not user data).

IKEv1 Phase 1 modes:

  • Main Mode — 6 messages. Provides identity protection (peer IDs are encrypted in messages 5-6). Slower but more secure. Default on most Cisco gear.
  • Aggressive Mode — 3 messages. Faster but exposes peer IDs in cleartext. Useful when one peer has a dynamic IP and needs to identify itself by name before encryption is possible.

IKEv2 (RFC 7296): newer version, replaces Main/Aggressive with a unified 4-message exchange (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH). Built-in NAT-T, EAP authentication, better handling of half-open sessions, rekey mechanism. Modern deployments increasingly use IKEv2.

Verification on IOS:

show crypto isakmp sa
show crypto ikev2 sa   (for IKEv2)

Look for state MM_ACTIVE (Main Mode done, Phase 1 up) or READY for IKEv2.

Exam framing: 'Phase 1 purpose' → build ISAKMP SA (management tunnel) + authenticate peers + DH key exchange. 'Phase 1 output' → one bidirectional SA.

hack.memory: Phase 1 = build the MANAGEMENT tunnel. Negotiate algorithms → DH key exchange → authenticate peers → ISAKMP SA (one bidirectional). IKEv1 Main Mode (6 msgs) or Aggressive (3 msgs). IKEv2 = 4 msgs unified.
hack.practice: On a lab router: `debug crypto isakmp`. Bring up a tunnel. Watch the 6-message Main Mode exchange step by step: SA proposal, DH values, authentication. Then `show crypto isakmp sa` — see MM_ACTIVE. Clear and redo to internalize.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Purpose of Phase 1?' → authenticate peers + build ISAKMP SA. 'Output of Phase 1?' → 1 bidirectional ISAKMP SA. Main Mode = 6, Aggressive = 3, IKEv2 = 4.

5.5.f.info:
IKE Phase 2 negotiates the IPsec Security Associations that protect actual user data. It runs inside the Phase 1 ISAKMP tunnel — so the negotiation itself is encrypted and authenticated by the Phase 1 SA.

What Phase 2 accomplishes:

  • IPsec transform negotiation — agree on ESP algorithms (cipher + HMAC) for the data SA. Phase 2 algorithms can differ from Phase 1.
  • Interesting traffic selectors — both sides confirm which subnets / ports are protected.
  • SA lifetimes — how long the SA is valid before rekey (default 3600 seconds or 4,608,000 KB on Cisco).
  • Optional: PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy) — perform a new DH exchange for Phase 2, so Phase 1 key compromise doesn't reveal past Phase 2 traffic.
  • Output: two unidirectional IPsec SAs — one for A→B traffic, one for B→A traffic. Each has its own SPI (Security Parameter Index).

Quick Mode (IKEv1 Phase 2): 3 messages, much faster than Phase 1 because the ISAKMP tunnel is already up. Message 1 proposes transforms + selectors. Message 2 accepts. Message 3 confirms.

IKEv2: doesn't use separate 'Quick Mode.' The initial IKE_AUTH exchange already sets up the first pair of child SAs. Additional SAs use CREATE_CHILD_SA (2 messages).

Why two unidirectional SAs? Each SA is simplex by design. This lets each direction have independent sequence numbers, independent rekey timing, and simplifies hardware acceleration. You always see them in pairs.

Verification:

show crypto ipsec sa

Output shows inbound SA (SPI X) and outbound SA (SPI Y), packet counters for each direction, transform set, and SA lifetimes.

Exam framing: 'Phase 2 purpose?' → negotiate IPsec SAs for data. 'How many SAs?' → 2 unidirectional. 'Quick Mode messages?' → 3.

steps: 2 unidirectional IPsec SAs up (data tunnel ready)

hack.memory: Phase 2 = build the DATA tunnel. Runs INSIDE the Phase 1 tunnel. Negotiates transforms + selectors + lifetimes → outputs 2 unidirectional IPsec SAs (one per direction). Quick Mode = 3 messages. PFS = optional re-DH for forward secrecy.
hack.practice: Continue the previous lab: after Phase 1 comes up, `debug crypto ipsec` and trigger interesting traffic. Watch Quick Mode (3 messages). Then `show crypto ipsec sa` — see inbound SPI + outbound SPI, two SAs, independent counters. This is the master diagram.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'How many IPsec SAs after Phase 2?' → 2 unidirectional. 'Which phase uses Quick Mode?' → Phase 2. 'PFS means?' → new DH per Phase 2 = perfect forward secrecy.

5.5.f.term: Phase 1 before Phase 2 — the order rule
5.5.f.info:
Phase 1 ALWAYS comes first. Phase 2 cannot happen without Phase 1, because Phase 2 negotiates inside the Phase 1 secure tunnel. If Phase 1 fails or is torn down, Phase 2 cannot proceed and any existing IPsec SAs go with it.

Why this order is required:

  • Phase 2 negotiation exchanges sensitive material (transform proposals, selectors, fresh keying if PFS). It must be protected in transit.
  • The Phase 1 ISAKMP SA provides that protection — it's the secure management channel.
  • Without Phase 1 first, Phase 2 messages would be exposed in cleartext on the public internet.

SA lifetimes — independent but coupled:

  • Phase 1 ISAKMP SA default lifetime: 86400 seconds (24 hours) on Cisco.
  • Phase 2 IPsec SA default lifetime: 3600 seconds (1 hour) or 4,608,000 KB, whichever first.
  • Typically Phase 2 rekeys multiple times within a single Phase 1 lifetime — the management tunnel stays up while the data keys rotate.
  • When Phase 1 lifetime expires, both peers renegotiate it and continue without dropping data traffic (if timing is right).

Failure modes to recognize:

  • Phase 1 never completes → Phase 2 never starts. Often caused by mismatched PSK, wrong peer IP, or firewall blocking UDP 500.
  • Phase 1 up but Phase 2 fails → check transform-set mismatch or crypto ACL mismatch (source/destination selectors differ between peers).
  • Both phases up but no traffic flows → routing issue, or crypto ACL too narrow, or NAT breaking ESP (need NAT-T).

Exam framing: 'Which phase is first?' → Phase 1. 'Why must Phase 1 be first?' → It builds the secure channel that Phase 2 uses.

hack.memory: Phase 1 ALWAYS before Phase 2. P2 runs inside P1 tunnel. P1 lifetime = 24h, P2 = 1h (rekeys more often). Fail P1 = no P2. Fail P2 with P1 up = transform/ACL mismatch.
hack.practice: Intentionally misconfigure to see each failure: wrong PSK → P1 never completes. Then fix PSK but mismatch crypto ACLs → P1 up, P2 fails. Then fix everything → both up, traffic flows. Three deliberate breaks cement the phases.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Phase 1 must complete before ___' → Phase 2. 'Why?' → Phase 2 uses Phase 1 tunnel for secure negotiation.

5.5.g.term: Phase 1 produces ONE bidirectional ISAKMP SA
5.5.g.info:
After IKE Phase 1 completes, the peers share exactly one bidirectional ISAKMP SA (also called an IKE SA). This single SA handles IKE management traffic in both directions — proposals, acceptances, authentication messages, keep-alives, rekey negotiations.

Bidirectional because: IKE management traffic is low-volume and conversational (request/response). There's no performance benefit to splitting it into two unidirectional SAs, and the simpler model is easier to implement and debug.

What the ISAKMP SA contains:

  • Cipher + hash + DH group + auth method chosen during negotiation.
  • Shared secret derived from DH (used to derive IKE encryption and integrity keys).
  • Lifetime (default 86400s on Cisco).
  • Peer IPs and identity information.

What it does NOT do:

  • Does NOT carry user data. User data goes through IPsec SAs (Phase 2 output).
  • Does NOT handle data plane encryption. That's ESP.

Verification output:

Router# show crypto isakmp sa
IPv4 Crypto ISAKMP SA
dst             src             state          conn-id
203.0.113.2     203.0.113.1     MM_ACTIVE          1001

State meanings:

  • MM_ACTIVE — Main Mode completed, Phase 1 up.
  • MM_NO_STATE / MM_SA_SETUP / MM_KEY_EXCH — intermediate states during Phase 1 negotiation.
  • AG_* — Aggressive Mode equivalents.

Seeing MM_ACTIVE confirms Phase 1 succeeded. Anything else means the negotiation got stuck.

hack.memory: Phase 1 output = 1 bidirectional ISAKMP SA. State MM_ACTIVE = P1 up. Lifetime 86400s default. Does NOT carry user data — only IKE management traffic.
hack.practice: `show crypto isakmp sa` on a router with a working tunnel. Identify: src, dst, state (should be MM_ACTIVE), conn-id. Then `show crypto isakmp sa detail` — see the lifetime, cipher, hash. That's the single bidirectional SA.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Number of ISAKMP SAs after P1?' → 1 bidirectional. 'What state means P1 up?' → MM_ACTIVE.

5.5.g.term: Phase 2 produces TWO unidirectional IPsec SAs
5.5.g.info:
After IKE Phase 2 (Quick Mode) completes, the peers share two unidirectional IPsec SAs — one for each direction of data flow. These are the SAs that ESP actually uses to encrypt user traffic.

Why unidirectional:

  • Each SA has its own SPI (Security Parameter Index) — a 32-bit identifier that the receiver uses to look up the right decryption keys and parameters.
  • Each direction can have independent sequence numbers for anti-replay.
  • Hardware acceleration is easier when SAs are simplex.
  • Rekey timing is per-direction; asymmetric traffic volumes can trigger different rekey times in each direction.

What each IPsec SA contains:

  • SPI (inbound) / remote SPI (outbound).
  • Transform: cipher (AES-256) + HMAC (SHA-256) + mode (tunnel/transport).
  • Encryption and integrity keys (per direction).
  • Sequence number counter (per direction).
  • Lifetime (default 3600s / 4,608,000 KB).
  • Traffic selectors (which packets match this SA).

Verification output (abbreviated):

Router# show crypto ipsec sa
interface: GigabitEthernet0/1
  local  ident (addr/mask/prot/port): (10.1.0.0/255.255.255.0/0/0)
  remote ident (addr/mask/prot/port): (10.2.0.0/255.255.255.0/0/0)
  current_peer 203.0.113.2
  #pkts encaps: 1200, #pkts decaps: 1180
  inbound esp sas:
    spi: 0xAAAA1111
  outbound esp sas:
    spi: 0xBBBB2222

You see both the inbound SPI (for packets arriving from the peer) and the outbound SPI (for packets leaving to the peer) — two separate SAs.

Exam framing: 'How many IPsec SAs are created in Phase 2?' → 2 unidirectional. 'What uniquely identifies each SA?' → SPI. 'Which direction do packet counters refer to?' → pkts encaps = outbound, pkts decaps = inbound.

hack.memory: Phase 2 output = 2 unidirectional IPsec SAs (one per direction). Each has its own SPI, keys, sequence number, lifetime. `show crypto ipsec sa` shows inbound SPI + outbound SPI side by side.
hack.practice: `show crypto ipsec sa` on a working tunnel. Identify the inbound SPI and outbound SPI — they are different. Note #pkts encaps (outbound) and #pkts decaps (inbound) counters. Pass asymmetric traffic — counters diverge.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Number of IPsec SAs after P2?' → 2 unidirectional. 'Identifier?' → SPI. 'Lifetime default?' → 3600s / 4.6M KB.

5.5.g.info:
Quick Mode is the name for IKEv1 Phase 2. It's 'quick' because it reuses the already-established Phase 1 ISAKMP tunnel — no new DH exchange is required unless PFS is enabled, and no new authentication is needed (the peers are already authenticated from Phase 1).

Quick Mode exchange (3 messages):

  1. Msg 1 (initiator): proposed transforms, traffic selectors, nonce, optional DH public value (if PFS).
  2. Msg 2 (responder): accepted transform, traffic selectors, nonce, optional DH public value.
  3. Msg 3 (initiator): HASH_3 — a keyed hash that confirms the negotiated parameters, acting as a liveness and commitment check.

After the three-message exchange, both peers have derived the IPsec SA keys and installed the two unidirectional SAs in their SADB (Security Association Database). Data traffic matching the traffic selectors will now be encrypted and forwarded through the tunnel.

Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS): optional. With PFS, each Phase 2 negotiation performs a fresh DH exchange, producing fresh key material not derived from the Phase 1 keys. If the Phase 1 keys are later compromised, past Phase 2 traffic remains protected because its keys came from independent DH runs.

crypto ipsec transform-set MYSET esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac
crypto map MYMAP 10 ipsec-isakmp
 set peer 203.0.113.2
 set transform-set MYSET
 set pfs group14
 match address CRYPTO_ACL

Rekey timing: when Phase 2 SAs approach their lifetime expiration, peers automatically run Quick Mode again to generate fresh IPsec SAs. The old SAs remain installed briefly to handle in-flight traffic, then are torn down. Rekeys are usually invisible to user traffic.

IKEv2 equivalent: IKEv2 replaces Quick Mode with the CREATE_CHILD_SA exchange (2 messages). The initial IKE_AUTH in IKEv2 Phase 1 already sets up the first child SA; CREATE_CHILD_SA is used for rekeying or additional SAs.

hack.memory: Quick Mode = 3-message Phase 2. Protected by Phase 1 tunnel. Optional PFS adds a new DH for fresh keys. Rekeys happen automatically when SA lifetime nears expiry. IKEv2 uses CREATE_CHILD_SA (2 msgs) instead.
hack.practice: Add `set pfs group14` to the crypto map on both peers. Clear IPsec SAs (`clear crypto sa`) and watch Phase 2 re-run — now with a new DH. Compare with PFS disabled: Phase 2 reuses Phase 1 keys.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Phase 2 mode name?' → Quick Mode. 'Messages?' → 3. 'PFS function?' → new DH per Phase 2 = past traffic protected even if Phase 1 keys leaked.

5.5.h.info:
Tunnel mode encrypts the entire original IP packet — IP header and all — and encapsulates it inside a brand-new IP packet addressed to the VPN peers. It's the default mode for site-to-site VPNs because it hides internal addressing from the public internet.

Packet structure (ESP in tunnel mode):

[ NEW outer IP header (proto 50) ]  ← routed on public net
[ ESP header (SPI, SeqNum) ]
[ original IP header ]              ← ENCRYPTED
[ original payload ]                ← ENCRYPTED
[ ESP trailer + padding ]           ← ENCRYPTED
[ ESP auth (HMAC) ]                 ← NOT encrypted (authenticates all above)

What an eavesdropper sees: the outer IP header (VPN gateway IPs, proto 50), the ESP header (SPI, sequence number), and encrypted bytes. They cannot see the original source/destination IPs, ports, or payload.

Why tunnel mode for site-to-site:

  • Internal hosts (10.1.0.0/24, 10.2.0.0/24) use private addresses that aren't routable on the internet. Tunnel mode hides them behind the public peer IPs.
  • Gateways do the encrypting, not the hosts — end hosts remain unaware of IPsec.
  • Source-to-destination pair is hidden; only gateway-to-gateway is visible.

Overhead: tunnel mode adds ~50-60 bytes of overhead per packet (new IP header 20 bytes + ESP header/trailer + HMAC). MTU reduction is real — typical effective MTU inside the tunnel ~1400 for a 1500 MTU underlay. If the inner packet is close to MTU, fragmentation or path MTU discovery becomes important.

Analogy: you put a sealed, labeled envelope (original IP packet) inside a new shipping box with the VPN gateway addresses on the outside. The courier sees only the shipping box; the sender and recipient inside remain private.

Exam framing: 'Which IPsec mode is default for site-to-site VPNs?' → tunnel. 'Which hides the original IP header?' → tunnel. 'Packet structure?' → new IP + ESP + (encrypted original IP + payload) + ESP trailer + auth.

hack.memory: Tunnel mode = whole original packet (IP header + payload) encrypted, wrapped in NEW outer IP. Default for site-to-site. Hides internal addressing. ~50-60 bytes overhead per packet.
hack.practice: Capture IPsec tunnel-mode traffic in Wireshark. Note outer IP shows gateway IPs only. ESP payload is opaque. Now decrypt with the session key (Wireshark supports ESP decryption with keys) → inner IP header and payload become visible.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Site-to-site default?' → tunnel mode. 'Hides original IPs?' → tunnel mode.

5.5.h.info:
The structural difference between tunnel and transport mode is what gets encrypted and whether a new IP header is added.

Transport mode (ESP):

[ ORIGINAL IP header ]             ← visible, not encrypted
[ ESP header ]
[ Original payload (TCP/UDP + data) ]  ← ENCRYPTED
[ ESP trailer ]
[ ESP auth (HMAC) ]

Tunnel mode (ESP):

[ NEW outer IP header ]
[ ESP header ]
[ Original IP header ]             ← ENCRYPTED
[ Original payload ]               ← ENCRYPTED
[ ESP trailer ]
[ ESP auth (HMAC) ]

Key differences:

  • Transport: original IP header stays visible. No new outer IP header. Encrypts only the payload (TCP/UDP + application data).
  • Tunnel: original IP header is encrypted with the payload. A new outer IP header is added with the VPN gateway addresses.

Overhead: transport mode adds ~30 bytes (ESP header + trailer + HMAC). Tunnel mode adds ~50-60 (same as transport plus a new 20-byte IP header).

Use-case separation:

  • Transport — host-to-host: two endpoints that both run IPsec directly protect their own traffic. Server-to-server encryption in a data center. Also used when IPsec protects a GRE tunnel (GRE itself provides the encapsulation; IPsec just encrypts).
  • Tunnel — gateway-to-gateway: VPN routers protect traffic on behalf of downstream hosts. Site-to-site VPNs. Remote access client-to-concentrator traffic (though IKEv2 often uses tunnel mode with client as 'gateway').

Both modes support both ESP and AH. Combinations: ESP transport, ESP tunnel, AH transport, AH tunnel. Only ESP is widely used; the modes distinguish what layer of protection is needed.

hack.memory: Tunnel = new outer IP + encrypts original IP + payload. Transport = keeps original IP visible + encrypts payload only. Tunnel for site-to-site (gateways). Transport for host-to-host or inside GRE.
hack.practice: Draw both packet structures side by side. Highlight what's encrypted (blue fill) and what's visible (grey). Transport = blue covers only the payload. Tunnel = blue covers the original IP + payload, with a new outer IP on top.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Site-to-site?' → tunnel. 'Host-to-host?' → transport. 'Which adds new IP header?' → tunnel.

5.5.h.term: Which mode hides the original IP header?
5.5.h.info:
Answer: tunnel mode. This is the single most common tunnel-vs-transport exam question.

Why this distinction matters:

  • Addressing privacy — if you don't want observers on the path to know the real source and destination of your traffic, you need tunnel mode. Transport mode leaves the original IP header in the clear.
  • Private-to-public traversal — internal private addresses (10.x.x.x, 192.168.x.x) cannot be routed on the public internet. Tunnel mode wraps them in a publicly routable outer header; transport mode does not.
  • Topology hiding — tunnel mode prevents an observer from mapping your internal topology by analyzing traffic patterns between specific internal hosts.

Side-by-side observability:

  • Transport mode capture: observer sees 'Host-A (10.1.1.5) talking to Host-B (10.2.2.100) on ESP'. They know who's talking, just not what they're saying.
  • Tunnel mode capture: observer sees 'VPN gateway-A (203.0.113.1) talking to VPN gateway-B (203.0.113.2) on ESP'. They have no idea which internal hosts are behind either gateway.

Why site-to-site VPNs default to tunnel mode:

  • They operate between gateways that serve many internal hosts — need to hide the internal addressing.
  • Internal addresses are typically RFC 1918 (non-routable) — need a public outer header.
  • Routing protocols and internal topology should not leak to the public internet.

Exam test patterns:

  • 'Which IPsec mode hides the original source and destination IP addresses?' → tunnel.
  • 'Which mode adds a new outer IP header?' → tunnel.
  • 'Which mode is used between a host and another host directly?' → transport.
  • 'Which mode is the default for site-to-site VPNs?' → tunnel.

hack.memory: TUNNEL mode hides the original IP. Transport does not. Memorize: Tunnel = Total hiding. Transport = payload-only. Tunnel is site-to-site default for a reason.
hack.practice: Capture a tunnel-mode ESP packet and a transport-mode ESP packet side by side. In tunnel, the visible IP addresses are gateways. In transport, the visible IP addresses are the actual endpoints. Seeing this in Wireshark makes it unforgettable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Which mode hides the IP header?' → tunnel. Always. This is a guaranteed question.

5.5.i.info:
For site-to-site VPNs, the default and correct answer is tunnel mode. This is the pairing tested on the CCNA exam: gateway-to-gateway IPsec = tunnel mode.

Why tunnel mode fits site-to-site perfectly:

  • Gateways encrypt on behalf of hosts — downstream hosts send plain traffic to the gateway. The gateway, not the host, performs IPsec. Tunnel mode lets the gateway wrap the original host-to-host packet in a new gateway-to-gateway packet.
  • Private addressing is preserved — inside the tunnel, the original private IP addresses remain intact (encrypted, but unchanged). The outer header uses the gateways' public IPs, which are routable on the internet.
  • Many-to-many traffic — one tunnel can carry traffic between any host on one side and any host on the other, as long as the crypto ACL permits it. Tunnel mode's single encrypted container holds whatever packet comes next.

IOS configuration cue: on Cisco gear, mode tunnel is the default under the transform set, so you don't usually have to type it. It's already tunnel.

crypto ipsec transform-set SITE2SITE esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac
 mode tunnel   ! default, often implicit

Reference use cases:

  • Branch-to-HQ over the internet.
  • On-prem to AWS/Azure VPC VPN.
  • Partner extranet (B2B site-to-site).
  • DMVPN (which uses tunnel mode inside mGRE).
  • DMVPN / FlexVPN hub-and-spoke.

Exam framing: every time the scenario mentions 'two offices,' 'branch to HQ,' 'always on between sites,' 'router-to-router,' answer tunnel mode.

hack.memory: Site-to-site = Tunnel mode. Default. Gateways encrypt, hosts unaware. Inner IP (private addresses) encrypted; outer IP (public gateway IPs) visible. Cisco transform set default is `mode tunnel`.
hack.practice: Configure a site-to-site IPsec tunnel on two routers. Leave the transform set default (tunnel mode). Capture on WAN — see gateway-to-gateway ESP. Generate host-to-host traffic inside — confirm it traverses the tunnel. Swap to `mode transport` and observe how it breaks (hosts aren't the peers).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Site-to-site uses which IPsec mode?' → tunnel. 'What mode is the default transform-set setting?' → tunnel.

5.5.i.info:
When the two IPsec endpoints are the actual data source and destination — not intermediate gateways — transport mode is appropriate. The endpoints' own addresses are already the outer addresses; no need to hide them.

Typical host-to-host scenarios:

  • Server-to-server encryption in a data center — two servers with IPsec capability encrypt direct traffic between themselves. Addresses are internal but already visible within the DC.
  • End-to-end security between two specific users — rare, but possible when both endpoints run IPsec stacks.
  • GRE protection — GRE already handles encapsulation (carrying multicast/routing). IPsec in transport mode just encrypts the GRE packet without adding another IP header (avoids double encapsulation overhead).
  • L2TP/IPsec remote access — legacy remote access stack where L2TP provides the tunnel and IPsec transport encrypts it.

Why not use tunnel mode for these cases?

  • You'd pay an extra 20-byte IP header overhead for no benefit — the source/destination are already the endpoints, there's nothing to hide.
  • Some stacks require transport mode for proper L2TP/GRE interoperation.

Configuration (rare in CCNA labs):

crypto ipsec transform-set HOSTMODE esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac
 mode transport

Exam framing: if the scenario says 'two hosts,' 'end-to-end,' 'servers protecting direct communication,' answer transport. If it says 'gateways,' 'routers,' 'offices,' answer tunnel.

Quick classification test:

  • Are the IPsec peers the actual source and destination of the user data? → Transport.
  • Are the IPsec peers intermediate gateways forwarding on behalf of other hosts? → Tunnel.

hack.memory: Host-to-host = Transport mode. Endpoints are the peers themselves. Original IP header stays visible. No new outer header (saves ~20 bytes). Also used inside GRE and with L2TP/IPsec.
hack.practice: Rare in CCNA labs but a good exercise: two Linux hosts with strongSwan configured in transport mode. Exchange traffic between them. Capture — note the original IP header is visible; only TCP/UDP + payload are encrypted.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Host-to-host?' → transport. 'Two servers encrypting direct traffic?' → transport. 'GRE protected by IPsec?' → typically transport.

5.5.j.info:
GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) is a Cisco-developed tunneling protocol, standardized as RFC 2784. It encapsulates any protocol inside an IP packet, including multicast, broadcast, non-IP (IPX, AppleTalk historically), and routing protocol traffic. GRE carries; it does not secure.

GRE header structure (simplified):

[ Outer IP header (proto 47) ]
[ GRE header (flags + protocol type) ]
[ Original (inner) packet — any protocol ]

Key properties:

  • IP protocol 47 — GRE is its own IP-layer protocol.
  • Stateless — no handshake, no state tables. Just wrap and send.
  • No encryption, no authentication — bare GRE is transparent and open to anyone on the path.
  • Can carry multicast — this is the critical feature. The inner packet can be a multicast frame (OSPF Hello at 224.0.0.5, EIGRP at 224.0.0.10), and GRE wraps it into a unicast GRE packet between the tunnel endpoints.
  • Point-to-point by default — each GRE tunnel is between two specific endpoints. mGRE (multipoint GRE) is the multipoint variant used in DMVPN.

Cisco IOS GRE tunnel config:

interface Tunnel0
 ip address 10.100.100.1 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/0
 tunnel destination 203.0.113.2
 tunnel mode gre ip   ! default, often implicit

Use cases:

  • Running OSPF or EIGRP between sites over a non-IPsec underlay.
  • Connecting two IPv6 islands over an IPv4 core (6-in-4 tunneling).
  • Simple branch connectivity when encryption is not required (rare today — most want encryption, hence GRE over IPsec).

Exam framing: 'What is GRE?' → tunneling protocol that can carry multicast and multiple L3 protocols. 'GRE encryption?' → none, use IPsec. 'IP protocol?' → 47.

hack.memory: GRE = tunneling, not security. IP proto 47. Carries multicast, broadcast, non-IP. Stateless. NO encryption. Modern use: inside IPsec (GRE over IPsec) to add encryption.
hack.practice: Configure a GRE tunnel between two routers without IPsec. Run OSPF over it → neighbors form because GRE carries multicast. Capture on WAN — see protocol 47 GRE packets, inner OSPF visible because no encryption. Now add IPsec → inner payload goes encrypted.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'GRE protocol number?' → 47. 'Does GRE encrypt?' → no. 'Why use GRE if IPsec exists?' → multicast support.

5.5.j.term: Why IPsec alone can't carry routing protocols
5.5.j.info:
Pure IPsec (ESP or AH without GRE wrapping) is unicast-only. It cannot encapsulate multicast or broadcast packets. This creates a fundamental problem: most dynamic routing protocols depend on multicast for neighbor discovery and update distribution.

Multicast addresses used by routing protocols:

  • OSPFv2: 224.0.0.5 (AllSPFRouters), 224.0.0.6 (AllDRouters)
  • EIGRP: 224.0.0.10 (EIGRP routers)
  • RIPv2: 224.0.0.9
  • PIM: 224.0.0.13
  • VRRP: 224.0.0.18
  • HSRP: 224.0.0.2 (v1), 224.0.0.102 (v2)

What happens when you try OSPF over a pure IPsec tunnel:

  • Router A sends OSPF Hello to 224.0.0.5 out its tunnel interface.
  • IPsec processes the Hello — but it's a multicast packet. IPsec crypto ACLs typically match unicast-only traffic, and the tunnel is point-to-point unicast-addressed. The multicast is dropped.
  • Neighbor relationship never forms. No OSPF routes cross the tunnel.

Workarounds (without GRE):

  • Static routes — manually configure routes pointing through the IPsec tunnel. Works but doesn't scale and doesn't adapt to failures.
  • OSPF neighbor statements — OSPF on some interfaces supports neighbor x.x.x.x which forces unicast Hellos. Viable in small deployments but still per-neighbor config.
  • BGP — BGP is inherently unicast (uses TCP 179). Works over IPsec natively. Many SD-WAN overlays use this.

The cleaner fix: wrap multicast inside GRE. GRE encapsulates the multicast OSPF Hello into a unicast GRE packet between the two tunnel endpoints. Then IPsec encrypts the GRE packet. Both problems solved — multicast works (because it's wrapped in unicast GRE) AND the traffic is encrypted (because IPsec protects the GRE).

hack.memory: Pure IPsec = unicast only = no multicast = no OSPF/EIGRP. Workarounds: static routes, BGP (unicast), or wrap in GRE. GRE over IPsec is the clean fix.
hack.practice: Configure OSPF on a pure IPsec tunnel between two routers → no neighbor. Now configure GRE over IPsec → OSPF neighbor comes up immediately. Capture the GRE packets — see the encrypted payload carrying the OSPF Hello.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Why can't OSPF run over plain IPsec?' → IPsec is unicast; OSPF uses multicast. 'Fix?' → GRE over IPsec.

5.5.j.info:
GRE over IPsec is the canonical solution when you need both the flexibility of GRE (multicast, routing protocols, multiple L3 protocols) and the security of IPsec (encryption, integrity, authentication). The layering is: original packet → GRE → IPsec → outer IP.

How the layering works:

  1. The router encapsulates the original packet (possibly multicast) into a GRE packet. The GRE packet is unicast between the two tunnel endpoints.
  2. The GRE packet is then protected by IPsec (typically ESP in transport mode, since GRE already provides its own outer header).
  3. The IPsec-protected GRE packet is routed across the public internet to the peer.
  4. Peer decrypts ESP → exposes GRE → de-GRE → original packet → routed into the destination LAN.

Two common IOS configurations:

  • Static crypto map with 'match address' permitting GRE — old school. Crypto ACL says `permit gre host A host B`.
  • IPsec profile on tunnel interface — modern. Apply an IPsec profile directly to the GRE tunnel interface: tunnel protection ipsec profile MYPROFILE. Cleaner, VTI-style.
! IPsec profile
crypto isakmp policy 10
 encr aes 256
 hash sha256
 authentication pre-share
 group 14
crypto isakmp key Cisco123 address 203.0.113.2
crypto ipsec transform-set TSET esp-aes 256 esp-sha256-hmac
 mode transport
crypto ipsec profile PROT_GRE
 set transform-set TSET

! GRE tunnel with IPsec protection
interface Tunnel0
 ip address 10.100.100.1 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/0
 tunnel destination 203.0.113.2
 tunnel protection ipsec profile PROT_GRE

Now run OSPF on Tunnel0 — neighbors form, routes exchange, everything encrypted.

Exam framing: 'How do you run OSPF over an IPsec VPN?' → GRE over IPsec. 'What does GRE add?' → multicast / routing protocol support. 'What does IPsec add?' → encryption / authentication.

hack.memory: GRE over IPsec = moving truck + armored escort. GRE wraps multicast into unicast. IPsec encrypts the GRE. Modern IOS: `tunnel protection ipsec profile NAME` on the tunnel interface. Enables OSPF/EIGRP over VPN.
hack.practice: Build it end-to-end: ISAKMP policy + PSK + transform-set (transport mode) + IPsec profile + GRE tunnel + `tunnel protection`. Verify: `show crypto ipsec sa`, `show ip ospf neighbor`. OSPF comes up; packets encrypted.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'OSPF over VPN?' → GRE over IPsec. Modern syntax uses `tunnel protection ipsec profile`.

5.5.j.info:
DMVPN (Dynamic Multipoint VPN) is Cisco's scalable hub-and-spoke VPN overlay. It combines three technologies — mGRE (multipoint GRE), NHRP (Next Hop Resolution Protocol), and IPsec — to let spokes dynamically build on-demand tunnels to each other without requiring full-mesh configuration.

The problem DMVPN solves: classic hub-and-spoke site-to-site has every branch tunnel to the HQ hub. Branch-to-branch traffic hairpins through HQ, wasting bandwidth and adding latency. Full mesh (every branch to every branch) doesn't scale — N branches need N(N-1)/2 tunnels, each statically configured. DMVPN gets the scaling of hub-and-spoke with the efficiency of mesh.

How DMVPN works:

  • Hub has an mGRE tunnel interface with one public IP endpoint.
  • Spokes each have an mGRE tunnel interface; they connect to the hub and register with NHRP.
  • NHRP maintains a mapping: spoke-tunnel-IP → spoke-public-IP. The hub is the NHRP server; spokes are NHRP clients.
  • When Spoke-A wants to talk to Spoke-B, it asks NHRP at the hub 'what is Spoke-B's public IP?' The hub answers. Spoke-A and Spoke-B then build a direct dynamic tunnel between themselves, protected by IPsec.
  • Traffic flows directly between spokes without hairpinning through the hub.

Phases of DMVPN:

  • Phase 1 — spokes only talk to hub. All spoke-to-spoke traffic hairpins. Simple to deploy.
  • Phase 2 — spoke-to-spoke dynamic tunnels enabled. Spokes resolve next-hop via NHRP and build direct tunnels.
  • Phase 3 — enhanced summarization, better scaling, recommended.

CCNA exposure: the exam expects you to recognize DMVPN as the Cisco hub-and-spoke VPN with dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels. You won't configure it from scratch, but know the components (mGRE + NHRP + IPsec) and that it solves the full-mesh scaling problem.

hack.memory: DMVPN = scalable hub-and-spoke + on-demand spoke-to-spoke. Three ingredients: mGRE (multipoint GRE) + NHRP (maps tunnel IP → public IP) + IPsec (encryption). Phase 1 = no spoke-to-spoke. Phase 2/3 = spoke-to-spoke direct tunnels.
hack.practice: Draw a 5-branch DMVPN: hub in middle, 4 spokes around. Label each spoke's persistent tunnel to the hub. Add a dashed on-demand tunnel between two spokes. Note NHRP query arrow going spoke → hub → back. This one diagram captures DMVPN.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam: 'Cisco hub-and-spoke VPN with dynamic spoke-to-spoke?' → DMVPN. 'Components?' → mGRE + NHRP + IPsec.

5.6.a.info:
A standard ACL filters IP traffic based on the source IP address only. It has no knowledge of the destination, protocol, or port — so it's a blunt tool that either allows or blocks all traffic from a given source regardless of where it's going.

Numbered ranges:

  • 1-99 — original range.
  • 1300-1999 — expanded range added later for organizations that needed more numbered ACLs.

Syntax (numbered):

access-list [1-99 | 1300-1999] {permit | deny} [source] [source-wildcard]

Examples:

  • access-list 10 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 — permits any source in 192.168.1.0/24.
  • access-list 10 permit host 10.1.1.5 — permits only 10.1.1.5.
  • access-list 10 deny any log — explicit deny any with logging.

Syntax (named):

ip access-list standard BLOCK_GUESTS
 deny 192.168.99.0 0.0.0.255
 permit any

Why standard ACLs are still useful despite the limitations:

  • Restrict who can SSH/Telnet to the device via access-class on VTY lines — you only care about source IP.
  • Simple blanket allow/deny at a network boundary where granularity isn't needed.
  • Input to route maps (e.g., for BGP filtering by source prefix).

Exam framing: 'Standard ACL matches on ___' → source IP only. 'Numbered range?' → 1-99 or 1300-1999. 'Ideal for?' → simple source-based filtering (VTY access-class, blanket allow/deny).

hack.memory: Standard ACL = source IP only. Numbers 1-99 or 1300-1999. Bluntest tool in the box — can't distinguish destinations or ports. Best for VTY access-class.
hack.practice: Lab: `access-list 10 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255` → apply to line vty with `access-class 10 in`. SSH from 192.168.1.x (works). SSH from 10.x.x.x (denied). Standard ACL + access-class is the textbook management-plane lock.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3 (Vol 2). Exam: 'Standard ACL matches?' → source IP only. Numbered 1-99 / 1300-1999.

5.6.a.term: Standard ACL placement — close to DESTINATION
5.6.a.info:
Rule: place standard ACLs as close to the DESTINATION as possible. Mnemonic: SD — Standard → Destination.

Why: a standard ACL can only see source IP. If you place it near the source, you block that source from reaching every destination downstream — not just the one you intended. By placing it near the destination, you block traffic only where it's not welcome while letting the source reach other destinations via different paths.

Worked example:

Topology: PC-A (10.0.0.50) — R1 — R2 — R3 — Server-B (192.168.1.100). R3 also has Server-C (172.16.0.200) on a different interface.

Goal: block PC-A from reaching Server-B. Allow PC-A to reach Server-C.

  • WRONG placement (near source, R1 inbound): access-list 10 deny host 10.0.0.50 on R1 inbound. Now PC-A can't reach ANYTHING past R1 — including Server-C. Over-broad.
  • CORRECT placement (near destination, R3 outbound toward Server-B): access-list 10 deny host 10.0.0.50, permit any applied ip access-group 10 out on R3's interface toward Server-B. PC-A blocked from Server-B only; Server-C still reachable.

Caveat: placing near the destination means the unwanted traffic consumes bandwidth across the whole path before being dropped. That's the price of standard ACLs — you trade bandwidth efficiency for placement correctness. If you need both correctness and early dropping, use an extended ACL (which can be placed near the source precisely because it can distinguish destinations).

Exam framing: 'Where do you place a standard ACL?' → close to destination. 'Why?' → it only matches source, so source-side placement would block legit traffic to other destinations.

hack.memory: Standard → Destination (SD). Source-only matching means source-side placement blocks too much. Place near the destination to be surgical. Price: wasted bandwidth on the path.
hack.practice: Draw it: source → R1 → R2 → R3 → two destinations branching off R3. Place the standard ACL once near source (breaks access to both) and once near destination (surgically blocks only one). See the difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Top-5 tested CCNA topic. Standard = Destination. Extended = Source. Memorize as an absolute.

5.6.a.term: Standard ACL syntax — numbered vs named
5.6.a.info:
Standard ACLs come in two syntactic flavors: numbered (old school, still tested) and named (modern, self-documenting). Both produce the same behavior; named is preferred for readability.

Numbered syntax:

access-list [1-99 | 1300-1999] {permit | deny} [source] [source-wildcard] [log]
access-list 10 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255
access-list 10 deny host 10.99.99.99
access-list 10 permit any

Each access-list 10 ... statement appends a new entry. To remove entries, you historically had to no access-list 10 (which deletes the whole ACL) and start over.

Named syntax:

ip access-list standard NAME_HERE
 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255
 deny host 10.99.99.99
 permit any
exit

Inside the sub-config mode, entries are typed without the access-list prefix. Sequence numbers are auto-assigned (10, 20, 30...) and can be manipulated surgically (no 20 to delete line 20; type 15 permit host ... to insert between 10 and 20).

Apply the ACL to an interface (either form):

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group 10 out

or for named:

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group NAME_HERE out

Apply to VTY (either form):

line vty 0 15
 access-class 10 in

Exam framing: know both syntaxes. Questions may present a numbered ACL and ask what it does, or ask you to configure a named standard ACL for a scenario. The keyword standard inside the named form is required — omitting it defaults to extended.

hack.memory: Standard ACL syntax: numbered (`access-list 10 permit ...`) or named (`ip access-list standard NAME` → sub-config). Apply: `ip access-group` on interface, `access-class` on VTY. Named + sequence numbers = modern.
hack.practice: Build both versions of the same standard ACL. Numbered: `access-list 10 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255`. Named: `ip access-list standard LAN_ONLY` → `permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255`. Verify with `show access-lists` — output looks the same.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Exam: both syntaxes appear. Know named standard ACL keyword placement.

5.6.a.info:
The host keyword is a syntactic shortcut that means 'match this exact IP address, every bit must match.' It's equivalent to writing the IP followed by a wildcard of 0.0.0.0.

Equivalence:

  • permit host 10.1.1.5 ≡ permit 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0
  • deny host 192.168.1.100 ≡ deny 192.168.1.100 0.0.0.0

Why the shortcut exists:

  • Readability — host 10.1.1.5 is easier to parse at a glance than 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0.
  • Convention — show access-lists output displays the host form for all /32 matches, so you'll see it more often than the explicit wildcard form.

Wildcard 0.0.0.0 explained: every bit is 0 = every bit must match. Since there are 32 bits in an IPv4 address, all 32 must match exactly. That's a /32 host route.

Common uses:

  • Permitting a single admin workstation to SSH: access-list 5 permit host 10.0.0.50, then access-class 5 in on VTY.
  • Denying a known malicious IP: deny host 198.51.100.42.
  • Restricting SNMP, NTP, or syslog to specific servers.

Exam framing: expect questions that switch between host X.X.X.X and X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0 syntax. They mean the same thing. Also expect recognition of host in show output as indicating a /32 match.

hack.memory: `host X.X.X.X` = shortcut for `X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0` = exact /32 match. IOS displays the `host` form in show output. Both syntax forms are interchangeable.
hack.practice: Configure both forms side by side: `permit 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0` and `permit host 10.1.1.5`. Run `show access-lists` — both display as `permit 10.1.1.5` or `permit host 10.1.1.5` (normalized to the shortcut).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam uses both interchangeably.

5.6.a.info:
The any keyword is the opposite shortcut to host: it means 'match every possible IP address — don't check any bits.' It's equivalent to writing 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255.

Equivalence:

  • permit any ≡ permit 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255
  • deny any ≡ deny 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255

Wildcard 255.255.255.255 explained: every bit is 1 = every bit is 'don't care.' Zero bits are checked. Every address matches. That's the match-all wildcard.

Very common uses:

  • Terminal permit — ACLs have an implicit deny at the end; if you want to block specific sources but allow everything else, end with permit any.
  • Blanket deny with logging — deny any log at the very end makes the implicit deny visible and triggers syslog on drops.
  • Extended ACLs — use any for source or destination when you don't want to constrain that side. permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 = allow HTTPS from any source to this server.

Exam framing: recognize that any and 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 are identical. Also know that every ACL has an implicit deny at the end — if you want to permit everything else after denying specific traffic, you must type permit any explicitly.

hack.memory: `any` = shortcut for `0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255` = match everything. 0 bits checked. Critical at the end of an ACL — `permit any` after specific denies; or `deny any log` to make implicit deny visible.
hack.practice: Build an ACL: `deny host 10.99.99.99` + `permit any`. Without the `permit any`, the implicit deny blocks everything. With it, only 10.99.99.99 is blocked. Swap the order and watch the behavior change.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: recognize `any` as 0.0.0.0/0 wildcard match-all.

5.6.b.term: Extended ACL — source + destination + protocol + port
5.6.b.info:
An extended ACL filters traffic on many criteria at once: source IP, destination IP, IP protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP/...), source port, destination port, TCP flags. This granularity makes them precision instruments — you can allow SSH from one subnet to one server while blocking HTTP from the same subnet to the same server.

Numbered ranges:

  • 100-199 — original range.
  • 2000-2699 — expanded range.

Full syntax (numbered):

access-list [100-199 | 2000-2699] {permit | deny} [protocol] [source] [src-wildcard] [operator src-port] [destination] [dst-wildcard] [operator dst-port] [flags] [log]

Examples:

  • access-list 100 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 80 — HTTP from 10.0.0.0/24 to server 192.168.1.100.
  • access-list 100 deny icmp any any echo — drop all inbound pings.
  • access-list 100 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 established — allow return HTTPS traffic to a server (only if TCP ACK bit set = existing connection).
  • access-list 100 deny udp any any range 0 1023 — block all well-known UDP ports.

Common protocols: ip (any IP traffic), tcp, udp, icmp, esp, ah, gre, ospf, eigrp.

Common ports (memorize): 20/21 FTP, 22 SSH, 23 Telnet, 25 SMTP, 53 DNS, 67/68 DHCP, 69 TFTP, 80 HTTP, 110 POP3, 123 NTP, 143 IMAP, 161 SNMP, 389 LDAP, 443 HTTPS, 445 SMB, 514 Syslog, 636 LDAPS, 993 IMAPS, 995 POP3S, 3389 RDP.

Exam framing: extended ACL syntax is heavily tested. Practice writing entries from scratch: protocol → source+wildcard → (optional src port) → destination+wildcard → (optional dst port).

hack.memory: Extended = source + dest + protocol + port + flags. Numbered 100-199 or 2000-2699. Precision tool. Syntax: `access-list N {permit|deny} PROTO SRC WILDCARD [op PORT] DST WILDCARD [op PORT]`.
hack.practice: Write from memory: 'permit HTTPS from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100'. Answer: `access-list 100 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 443`. Now 'block all FTP to that same server': `access-list 100 deny tcp any host 192.168.1.100 range 20 21`. Drill 10 scenarios.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Extended ACL syntax is guaranteed exam content. Practice until writing full commands is automatic.

5.6.b.term: Extended ACL placement — close to SOURCE
5.6.b.info:
Rule: place extended ACLs as close to the SOURCE as possible. Mnemonic: ES — Extended → Source.

Why: extended ACLs can precisely identify the exact traffic to block (source, destination, protocol, port). There's no risk of collateral damage from blocking too much. Placing them near the source drops unwanted traffic immediately, saving bandwidth on every link between source and destination.

Worked example:

Topology: 10.0.0.0/24 users — R1 — R2 — R3 — 192.168.1.100 server.

Goal: block HTTP (TCP 80) from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100. Allow all other traffic (HTTPS, SSH, ping).

  • CORRECT (near source, R1 inbound): access-list 100 deny tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 80 + permit ip any any, applied ip access-group 100 in on R1's interface toward users. Unwanted HTTP packets die on R1 and never waste bandwidth on R1-R2, R2-R3 links.
  • WORKS BUT WASTEFUL (near destination): same ACL on R3 outbound toward the server. Same logical result, but packets travel all the way across the network before being dropped. Bandwidth wasted.

Why it works: because the extended ACL explicitly specifies the destination (host 192.168.1.100) and the protocol (TCP 80), it can selectively block only that traffic without affecting HTTPS, SSH, or traffic to other destinations. Standard ACLs can't do this because they don't know the destination.

Combined rule: Standard → Destination, Extended → Source (SD / ES). Memorize as one block. Nearly every CCNA ACL-placement question reduces to identifying ACL type + applying this rule.

hack.memory: Extended → Source (ES). Precision means you can kill bad traffic immediately without collateral damage. Saves bandwidth across the whole path. Combined rule: SD + ES.
hack.practice: Draw the same topology from the standard ACL example. This time place an extended ACL. Source-side (correct) = traffic dies immediately. Destination-side (wasteful) = traffic crosses the whole network before dropping. Walk through each to feel the difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Standard = Destination, Extended = Source. Memorize this pair.

5.6.b.term: Extended ACL full syntax and flags
5.6.b.info:
Writing an extended ACL correctly requires placing each element in the right order. The IOS parser is strict.

Full order of elements:

access-list [N] {permit|deny} [protocol] [src] [src-wildcard] [op src-port] [dst] [dst-wildcard] [op dst-port] [tcp-flags] [log]

Element by element:

  1. ACL number or name.
  2. Action — permit or deny.
  3. Protocol — ip, tcp, udp, icmp, protocol number, or named protocol.
  4. Source — IP + wildcard, or host X.X.X.X, or any.
  5. Source port (optional) — only valid with tcp/udp. Operators: eq, gt, lt, neq, range N M.
  6. Destination — IP + wildcard, or host, or any.
  7. Destination port (optional) — same operators.
  8. TCP flags (optional) — established (ACK or RST set), syn, ack, fin, etc.
  9. log (optional) — generate syslog on match. Performance cost; use sparingly.

Named extended ACL syntax:

ip access-list extended MY_ACL
 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 443
 deny tcp any host 192.168.1.100 eq 80
 permit ip any any
exit

Common pitfalls:

  • Forgetting wildcard mask — every IP except host or any must be followed by a wildcard.
  • Wrong protocol — using ip when you mean tcp means the port clause can't be added.
  • Source port vs destination port — source port goes right after the source IP, destination port after the destination IP.
  • Missing implicit deny — forgetting a permit any terminator silently blocks everything not explicitly permitted.

hack.memory: Order matters: number → action → proto → SRC + wildcard → src port → DST + wildcard → dst port → flags → log. Practice this order until writing full commands is reflex.
hack.practice: Drill: write 10 full extended ACEs from scratch. Include: HTTP allow, HTTPS allow, SSH allow from specific /24, DNS allow (UDP 53), ping deny (icmp echo), established TCP return traffic. Run each on a lab and verify with `show access-lists`.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam will test exact syntax. Know element order by heart.

5.6.b.term: Port operators — eq, gt, lt, neq, range
5.6.b.info:
Extended ACLs support several port match operators to express port conditions more flexibly than just 'equal to N.' You'll see all of these on the exam.

The five operators:

  • eq N — equal to port N. Most common. eq 80 matches exactly port 80.
  • gt N — greater than N. gt 1023 matches all ephemeral ports (1024-65535).
  • lt N — less than N. lt 1024 matches all well-known ports (0-1023).
  • neq N — not equal to N. Less common. neq 80 matches everything except port 80.
  • range N M — inclusive range from N to M. range 20 21 matches FTP data + control (both ports).

Examples:

  • permit tcp any host 10.1.1.1 eq 443 — HTTPS to one server.
  • deny tcp any any range 6000 6099 — block X11 range.
  • permit tcp any host 10.1.1.1 gt 1023 — permit ephemeral port return traffic.
  • permit udp any host 10.1.1.1 eq 53 — DNS to server.
  • permit tcp any host 10.1.1.1 range 20 22 — permits FTP data (20), FTP control (21), SSH (22).

Named ports: IOS accepts common port names instead of numbers — eq www, eq https, eq ssh, eq telnet, eq dns (UDP 53), eq domain, eq ntp, eq smtp, etc. Names are translated to numbers in the stored config, so eq 80 and eq www are identical.

Tricky flags alongside ports:

  • established — matches TCP packets with ACK or RST flag set (part of an existing connection). Useful for 'only allow return traffic.'
  • syn, ack, fin, rst — match specific TCP flag bits.

Exam framing: expect questions that use each operator. Read carefully — gt 1023 is very different from gt 1024.

hack.memory: Port operators: eq, gt, lt, neq, range N M. Named ports work too (www, https, ssh). `established` flag matches ACK/RST for return traffic. Drill each operator with examples.
hack.practice: Write these: allow all ephemeral return TCP to 10.1.1.100 (`gt 1023`). Block X11 range (`range 6000 6099`). Allow only SSH and RDP (multiple lines or `range 22 22` + `range 3389 3389`). Each operator needs practice.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Exam uses all 5 operators. Know named-port equivalents for www/https/ssh/telnet/dns/smtp/ntp.

5.6.c.info:
Named ACLs replace numeric identifiers with descriptive text names, making configurations self-documenting and easier to manage at scale. A 50-rule ACL called BLOCK_GUEST_INTERNET is instantly more understandable than access-list 150.

Syntax:

ip access-list {standard | extended} NAME_HERE
 [sub-config commands...]
exit

The standard or extended keyword is required — it tells IOS what type of ACL this is. Omitting it defaults to extended but is bad practice.

Inside the sub-config mode:

  • Type permit or deny directly — no access-list N prefix.
  • IOS auto-assigns sequence numbers in increments of 10 (10, 20, 30...).
  • Use no [seq#] to delete a specific entry without removing the whole ACL.
  • Type a specific sequence number before the rule to insert at that position.

Example — blocking guest subnet from internal resources while allowing internet:

ip access-list extended GUEST_POLICY
 deny ip 192.168.99.0 0.0.0.255 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255
 deny ip 192.168.99.0 0.0.0.255 172.16.0.0 0.15.255.255
 permit ip any any
exit

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group GUEST_POLICY in

Advantages over numbered:

  • Self-documenting — the name explains intent.
  • Easier debugging — `show access-lists` output shows the name.
  • Sequence-number editing works cleanly (newer IOS versions added this to numbered ACLs too, but it's cleaner with named).
  • No range constraints — no worry about running out of 100-199 slots.

Best practice: use ALL_CAPS_WITH_UNDERSCORES for ACL names — visually distinct and avoids collision with reserved keywords.

hack.memory: Named ACL = `ip access-list {standard|extended} NAME` → sub-config mode. Entries typed without `access-list` prefix. Auto-assigned sequence numbers. Self-documenting. Modern best practice.
hack.practice: Build: `ip access-list extended DENY_TELNET` → `deny tcp any any eq 23` → `permit ip any any` → exit. Apply: `ip access-group DENY_TELNET in` on an interface. `show access-lists` reveals the ACL with auto sequence numbers.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Named ACLs are modern default. Exam expects fluency in the syntax.

5.6.c.info:
Sequence numbers are line numbers assigned to each ACE (Access Control Entry) in an ACL. Modern IOS auto-assigns them in increments of 10 (10, 20, 30...) when entries are added. Sequence numbers enable editing individual entries without recreating the whole ACL.

Editing operations:

  • Delete an entry — enter the named-ACL sub-config, then no [seq#].
  • Insert an entry — type the desired sequence number at the start of a new rule. For example, to insert between 10 and 20, use sequence 15.
  • Re-number (resequence) — ip access-list resequence NAME START INCREMENT renumbers all entries cleanly.

Example edit session:

! Starting ACL:
ip access-list extended MY_ACL
 10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
 20 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
 30 permit ip any any

! Insert a new permit for SSH between 10 and 20
ip access-list extended MY_ACL
 15 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 22
exit

! Result:
 10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
 15 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 22
 20 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
 30 permit ip any any

Why this matters:

  • Before sequence numbers, any change required removing the ACL, making edits in a text editor, and reapplying — which briefly left interfaces unprotected and risked typos.
  • Now you can edit live without removing the ACL from the interface.
  • Named ACLs have supported sequence-number editing since IOS 12.0; numbered ACLs added it around 12.2.

Show output:

Router# show access-lists MY_ACL
Extended IP access list MY_ACL
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 (12345 matches)
    15 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 22
    20 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80 (0 matches)
    30 permit ip any any (6789 matches)

The match counter per ACE is invaluable for troubleshooting — a rule with 0 matches may indicate wrong order or the traffic not flowing as expected.

hack.memory: Sequence numbers = line numbers. Default auto-assign 10, 20, 30. Insert: type `15 permit ...` inside sub-config. Delete: `no 20`. Renumber: `ip access-list resequence NAME 10 10`. Match counters help troubleshoot.
hack.practice: Build a 3-entry named ACL. Insert a new entry between 10 and 20 by typing `15 permit ...`. Delete entry 20 with `no 20`. Resequence: `ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 100 10`. Watch the numbers change. This feature is invaluable in production.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Exam: 'how to delete one ACL entry without removing the whole ACL?' → enter named-ACL sub-config + `no [seq#]`.

5.6.c.info:
The ip access-list resequence command renumbers all ACEs in an ACL, starting from a new base and using a new increment. It's the cleanup tool for ACLs where you've inserted many entries and run out of room between existing sequence numbers.

Syntax:

ip access-list resequence NAME START_SEQ INCREMENT

Examples:

  • ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 10 10 — renumber starting at 10 in increments of 10 (the default state of a fresh ACL).
  • ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 100 20 — renumber starting at 100 in increments of 20 (gives lots of room to insert later).
  • ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 1000 100 — renumber starting at 1000 in large steps.

When to use it:

  • You've inserted 5 rules between 10 and 20 using sequences 11, 12, 13, 14, 15 — now you need to insert another between the original 10 and the first insertion. Resequence with larger increments.
  • You're about to merge two similar ACLs and want consistent numbering.
  • Cleanup before handing off an ACL to another admin.

Process walkthrough:

! Current (messy):
 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 18, 20

! Run: ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 10 10

! Result (clean):
 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70

Is this tested on CCNA? Yes, occasionally — know the command syntax and when it's useful. Resequencing preserves the order and content of ACEs; it only changes their sequence numbers.

hack.memory: `ip access-list resequence NAME START INCREMENT` = renumber all ACEs. Useful after many inserts fill up the gaps. Preserves order and content; only changes sequence numbers.
hack.practice: Insert 4 entries between 10 and 20 (11, 12, 13, 14, 15) in a named ACL. Try to insert another between 10 and 11 → no sequence gap. Run `ip access-list resequence NAME 10 10` → sequences become 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60. Gaps restored.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Know the command syntax: `ip access-list resequence NAME START INCREMENT`.

5.6.d.term: Wildcard mask — inverse of subnet mask
5.6.d.info:
A wildcard mask in an ACL tells the router which bits of the IP address must match exactly and which bits are 'don't care.' It is the inverse of a subnet mask: where a subnet mask uses 1s for 'network' and 0s for 'host,' a wildcard mask uses 0 for 'must match' and 1 for 'don't care.'

Rule in binary:

  • Bit = 0 → that bit position must match the given IP exactly.
  • Bit = 1 → that bit position is ignored (can be 0 or 1).

Classic conversions (subnet mask → wildcard):

  • /24 = 255.255.255.0 → wildcard 0.0.0.255 (match first 24 bits, last 8 don't care)
  • /16 = 255.255.0.0 → 0.0.255.255
  • /8 = 255.0.0.0 → 0.255.255.255
  • /25 = 255.255.255.128 → 0.0.0.127
  • /26 = 255.255.255.192 → 0.0.0.63
  • /27 = 255.255.255.224 → 0.0.0.31
  • /28 = 255.255.255.240 → 0.0.0.15
  • /29 = 255.255.255.248 → 0.0.0.7
  • /30 = 255.255.255.252 → 0.0.0.3
  • /32 = 255.255.255.255 → 0.0.0.0 (exact host)
  • /0 = 0.0.0.0 → 255.255.255.255 (match all)

Advanced: non-contiguous wildcards. Unlike subnet masks (which must be contiguous 1s), wildcard masks can have any bit pattern. A wildcard of 0.0.0.254 (binary 11111110) with IP 0.0.0.0 matches only even numbers. Rarely used in CCNA but theoretically legal.

Match test: to check whether IP X matches A.B.C.D wildcard W.X.Y.Z, compute (X XOR A) AND (NOT W). If result = 0, it matches.

hack.memory: Wildcard = inverse of subnet mask. 0 = MUST match. 1 = DON'T CARE. /24 = 0.0.0.255. /30 = 0.0.0.3. /28 = 0.0.0.15. Non-contiguous wildcards are legal (rare).
hack.practice: Speed drill: convert every /N to its wildcard under 60 seconds. Test yourself on /24, /16, /28, /30, /27, /25, /32, /0. Reverse: wildcard 0.0.0.63 = /26. Wildcard 0.0.3.255 = /22. Convert until instantaneous.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Wildcard math is tested. Master the conversion: subnet mask + wildcard = 255.255.255.255 (255 per octet).

5.6.d.term: Quick math — 255 minus each subnet-mask octet
5.6.d.info:
The fastest way to convert a subnet mask to a wildcard mask is to subtract each octet from 255. This works because subnet mask + wildcard mask always adds to 255.255.255.255 (all bits accounted for).

The formula:

wildcard_octet = 255 - subnet_mask_octet

Apply to each of the four octets independently.

Worked examples:

  • 255.255.255.0 → 0.0.0.255 (255-255=0, 255-255=0, 255-255=0, 255-0=255)
  • 255.255.255.192 → 0.0.0.63 (255-192=63)
  • 255.255.255.224 → 0.0.0.31 (255-224=31)
  • 255.255.0.0 → 0.0.255.255
  • 255.255.252.0 → 0.0.3.255 (255-252=3, 255-0=255)

Prefix length to wildcard shortcut: memorize these four octet-level conversions — any prefix length is a combination of them.

  • /24 → 0.0.0.255 (last octet fully open)
  • /25 → 0.0.0.127 (255-128)
  • /26 → 0.0.0.63 (255-192)
  • /27 → 0.0.0.31 (255-224)
  • /28 → 0.0.0.15 (255-240)
  • /29 → 0.0.0.7 (255-248)
  • /30 → 0.0.0.3 (255-252)

Multi-octet prefixes:

  • /20 → mask 255.255.240.0 → wildcard 0.0.15.255
  • /22 → mask 255.255.252.0 → wildcard 0.0.3.255
  • /14 → mask 255.252.0.0 → wildcard 0.3.255.255

Reverse: wildcard to prefix. Subtract wildcard octet from 255 to get subnet mask octet, then count the 1-bits for prefix length. Wildcard 0.0.0.15 → mask 255.255.255.240 → /28.

Exam tip: practice until you can convert both directions in under 10 seconds per problem. This is pure arithmetic that frees up mental bandwidth for the conceptual parts of ACL questions.

hack.memory: Wildcard = 255 minus subnet-mask octet. Per octet. Memorize 0/128/192/224/240/248/252 → 255/127/63/31/15/7/3. Practice until instant.
hack.practice: Paper drill: 10 random prefixes from /8 to /30. Convert each to both subnet mask and wildcard. Check with: subnet + wildcard = 255.255.255.255 per octet. Stop timing at 60 seconds total.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: wildcard conversions appear in nearly every ACL question. Drill to automatic.

5.6.d.info:
The wildcard 0.0.0.0 means 'every bit must match.' Paired with an IP address, it matches only that exact IP — a /32 host match. This is the wildcard behind the host keyword shortcut.

Why 0.0.0.0 means exact match: every bit is 0 → every bit must match → all 32 bits checked → the IP must be identical. That's /32.

Equivalence:

  • permit 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0 ≡ permit host 10.1.1.5
  • deny 192.168.1.100 0.0.0.0 ≡ deny host 192.168.1.100

Opposite: match-all wildcard (255.255.255.255). Every bit is 1 → every bit is don't-care → nothing is checked → every IP matches. That's /0. Equivalent to the any keyword.

  • permit 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 ≡ permit any

Four key wildcards to memorize:

WildcardMeaningShortcutPrefix
0.0.0.0Exact hosthost X.X.X.X/32
0.0.0.255Any /24 subnet(none)/24
0.0.255.255Any /16 network(none)/16
255.255.255.255Any IP at allany/0

Show output convention: IOS normalizes /32 wildcards to the host keyword and /0 wildcards to any when displaying. So even if you type 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0, show access-lists will print it as host 10.1.1.5.

Exam framing: recognize 0.0.0.0 wildcard as exact host (same as host X.X.X.X) and 255.255.255.255 wildcard as match-all (same as any).

hack.memory: 0.0.0.0 wildcard = /32 exact host (= `host` keyword). 255.255.255.255 wildcard = /0 match-all (= `any`). IOS normalizes show output to shortcut form.
hack.practice: Write: `permit 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0`. Run `show access-lists` — it displays as `permit host 10.1.1.5`. Write `deny 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255` → shows as `deny any`. IOS converts both directions invisibly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam pairs `host X` ↔ `X 0.0.0.0` and `any` ↔ `0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255`.

5.6.e.info:
The host keyword is the syntactic shortcut for matching exactly one IP address. Writing host 10.1.1.5 is identical in every way to writing 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0 — same match behavior, same show output (normalized to host form).

Why IOS has both forms:

  • Compactness — host 10.1.1.5 is one logical unit; 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0 is two tokens plus mental wildcard translation.
  • Readability — in a long ACL, host entries stand out clearly as single-host rules.
  • Consistency with show output — the router normalizes /32 entries to the host form when displaying ACLs.

Common scenarios using host:

  • Admin workstation access — access-list 5 permit host 10.0.0.50 + access-class 5 in on VTY locks down management access to one IP.
  • Specific server rules — access-list 100 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 — HTTPS to one server.
  • Known bad IP blocking — deny ip host 198.51.100.42 any log at network edge.

Positioning in an extended ACE: host can appear as the source or the destination. In fact, an extended ACE might have host in both positions:

access-list 100 permit tcp host 10.0.0.50 host 192.168.1.100 eq 22

This allows SSH from admin workstation 10.0.0.50 to server 192.168.1.100 — no one else, nothing else.

Exam framing: know that host X.X.X.X and X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0 are identical. The exam uses both — sometimes in the same question — and expects you to recognize them as equivalent.

hack.memory: `host X.X.X.X` = shortcut for `X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0` = exact /32 match. Both forms valid. Show output normalizes to `host`. Use `host` for readability in new ACLs.
hack.practice: Write three ACEs using `host` at different positions: as source only (`host 10.0.0.1 any`), as destination only (`any host 192.168.1.100`), and both (`host 10.0.0.1 host 192.168.1.100`). Each is common and distinct.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: `host X.X.X.X` ↔ `X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0` interchangeable.

5.6.e.info:
The any keyword is the syntactic shortcut for 'match every IP address.' It is identical to 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 and is the match-anything wildcard.

Equivalence:

  • permit any ≡ permit 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255
  • deny ip any any ≡ deny ip 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255

Most common uses:

  • Terminal permit — after denying specific traffic, add permit any (standard) or permit ip any any (extended) so the implicit deny doesn't block everything.
  • Explicit deny any log — deny ip any any log at the end makes the implicit deny visible and logs each dropped packet to syslog for troubleshooting.
  • Unconstrained source or destination — in extended ACEs, when the source or destination shouldn't matter, use any. Example: permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 allows HTTPS from any source.

Position flexibility: any works in either the source or destination slot of an extended ACE. You can use it on one side, both sides, or not at all:

access-list 100 permit tcp any any eq 443          ! anyone → anyone, HTTPS only
access-list 100 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443  ! anyone → server
access-list 100 permit tcp host 10.0.0.50 any eq 443   ! admin → anyone

Show output convention: IOS normalizes /0 wildcards to any in show output. Type 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255; it prints as any.

Exam framing: recognize any as the match-all keyword. Know that every ACL needs at least one permit (often permit any / permit ip any any) or everything is blocked by the implicit deny.

right.label: Common contexts
items: deny any log → visible implicit deny

hack.memory: `any` = match everything = `0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255` wildcard = /0. Critical at the end of ACLs to avoid total blocking by implicit deny. Show output normalizes to `any`.
hack.practice: Build this trap-ACL: `access-list 10 deny host 10.99.99.99` (no permit any after). Apply and test — ALL traffic is blocked, not just 10.99.99.99. Add `permit any` — now only 10.99.99.99 is blocked. This cements the implicit-deny lesson.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam always tests `any` = /0 match-all. Know implicit deny requires explicit `permit any` terminator.

5.6.e.term: Both syntax forms are valid and interchangeable
5.6.e.info:
The CCNA exam frequently uses both host X.X.X.X and X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0 (and similarly any vs 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255) in the same question. You must read both forms fluently and recognize them as identical.

Interchangeable pairs:

  • permit host 10.1.1.5 ≡ permit 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0
  • permit any ≡ permit 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255
  • deny host 192.168.1.1 ≡ deny 192.168.1.1 0.0.0.0

How to read ACLs fluently:

  1. For every IP in an ACE, read the wildcard mask (or keyword) next to it.
  2. If wildcard = 0.0.0.0 (or keyword = host), it's an exact /32 match.
  3. If wildcard = 255.255.255.255 (or keyword = any), it matches everything.
  4. Otherwise, compute the prefix length: 255 - each wildcard octet gives the subnet mask, then count the 1-bits.

Practice paragraph: Given the ACE access-list 100 permit tcp host 10.0.0.50 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 eq 80, what does it mean?

  • Action: permit.
  • Protocol: TCP.
  • Source: host 10.0.0.50 (exactly 10.0.0.50, nothing else).
  • Destination: 192.168.1.0/24 (any IP in that /24).
  • Destination port: 80 (HTTP).
  • Result: 10.0.0.50 is allowed to HTTP to anything in 192.168.1.0/24.

Exam strategy: when you see a wildcard mask you don't immediately recognize, compute it on scratch paper. Never guess. A single misread wildcard can flip 'permit' into 'block' in the answer.

hack.memory: Read both forms fluently. host X = X 0.0.0.0. any = 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255. Exam uses both, sometimes mixed. Compute wildcard → prefix on scratch paper for unfamiliar masks.
hack.practice: Translate 10 random ACEs back and forth: given shortcut, write explicit wildcard form. Given explicit, write shortcut. After 10 reps, both forms feel equivalent in your head.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3-4 (Vol 2). Critical exam skill: recognize both forms instantly.

5.6.f.term: Implicit deny any — the invisible last line
5.6.f.info:
Every Cisco ACL ends with an invisible, automatic deny any rule. This rule is not displayed in show access-lists output and is not configurable — it is always present and always enforced. If a packet doesn't match any explicit permit or deny entry, it hits the implicit deny and is dropped.

Implications:

  • An ACL with only deny entries blocks ALL traffic — the explicit denies block what they match, the implicit deny catches everything else.
  • An ACL with one permit is effectively a whitelist — that one flow is allowed, everything else is denied by the implicit rule.
  • Every ACL MUST have at least one explicit permit or no traffic will flow.
  • To allow 'everything except specific bad stuff,' you must explicitly add permit any (standard) or permit ip any any (extended) at the end — after your deny entries.

Visualization:

ip access-list extended MY_ACL
 10 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 23   ! block Telnet
 20 permit ip any any                    ! allow everything else
(implicit: deny ip any any)              ! NEVER DISPLAYED, still enforced

Without line 20, what happens? Everything except the denied Telnet would still be blocked because the implicit deny catches it. Adding permit ip any any is essential to allow all other traffic.

The implicit deny does NOT increment a counter visibly. Since it's not a real ACE, show access-lists won't show matches against it. This makes troubleshooting tricky — 'why is my traffic being blocked?' often answers 'it's hitting the implicit deny because no explicit permit matches.'

Exam framing: the implicit deny is one of the most commonly tested ACL concepts. Expect scenarios where the ACL only has denies (or only has narrow permits) and you must recognize that all other traffic is dropped.

hack.memory: Every ACL ends with an INVISIBLE deny any. If no explicit permit matches, traffic is dropped. Every ACL needs at least one `permit` or everything is blocked. Implicit deny is not displayed in show output.
hack.practice: Build an ACL with ONLY deny entries: `access-list 10 deny host 10.99.99.99`. Apply it. Try to send traffic from ANY other source → blocked. Now add `access-list 10 permit any` → other traffic works, 10.99.99.99 still blocked. The invisible rule reveals itself through behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Most tested ACL concept. Know: every ACL ends with hidden `deny any`. You must include a `permit any` terminator to allow other traffic.

5.6.f.term: Explicit `deny any log` — making the implicit visible
5.6.f.info:
Because the implicit deny is invisible in show access-lists output and doesn't increment match counters, troubleshooting dropped traffic can be hard. Adding an explicit deny ip any any log at the end of every ACL solves both problems.

What it does:

  • Behavior is identical to the implicit deny — packets not matching earlier entries are dropped.
  • Match counter is visible — show access-lists now shows how many packets hit this line.
  • Syslog messages — the log keyword generates a syslog entry for each match (summarized to avoid flooding: typically one entry per 5 minutes per unique flow, plus a count).

Example:

ip access-list extended WEB_ONLY
 10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
 20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
 30 deny ip any any log   ! explicit, counted, logged
exit

Now run show access-lists WEB_ONLY:

Extended IP access list WEB_ONLY
 10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 (3456 matches)
 20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80 (234 matches)
 30 deny ip any any log (89 matches)

The 89 matches on line 30 tell you 89 packets were blocked. Syslog messages reveal which sources attempted what.
