Performance warning: the log keyword forces packets to process-switch (punt to CPU) instead of CEF-switch (hardware). On high-traffic links, this can stress the router CPU. Use sparingly in production; use generously in lab/small-scale troubleshooting.

Alternative: log-input — like log but also records the ingress interface and source MAC. Useful for tracing back to a specific switch port.

Exam framing: know that explicit deny any log is optional but recommended for visibility and troubleshooting. The behavior is identical to implicit deny; the benefit is observability.

hack.memory: `deny ip any any log` at the end of every ACL → same behavior as implicit, but counter visible in show + syslog on matches. Performance cost: punts packets to CPU. Use in troubleshooting / small deployments.
hack.practice: Compare two ACLs: one ending implicitly, one ending with `deny ip any any log`. Send blocked traffic to both. Run `show access-lists` — only the explicit version shows the counter. Check syslog (`show logging`) — only the explicit version logs drops.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Exam may ask about `log` keyword. Know: log generates syslog, punts to CPU, useful for troubleshooting.

5.6.f.info:
The most common ACL mistake — and a frequent exam scenario — is an ACL that has only specific permit rules and no terminating permit any. Every bit of traffic not explicitly permitted is silently dropped by the implicit deny.

The trap ACL:

access-list 100 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 80
!(implicit: deny ip any any)

What breaks:

  • HTTP from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100 → works (matches line 1).
  • HTTPS from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100 → blocked (no match, hits implicit deny).
  • DNS from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100 → blocked.
  • Ping from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100 → blocked.
  • Traffic from 10.1.0.0/24 (different subnet) → blocked.
  • Return traffic from the server → blocked on the other direction if that direction has a similar ACL.

The fix: add a terminator.

access-list 100 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 80
access-list 100 permit ip any any    ! the critical terminator

Now HTTP is allowed specifically (matches line 1), everything else is allowed generically (matches line 2), and nothing hits the implicit deny.

Exam scenarios to watch for:

  • ACL shows specific permits, no terminator. Question: 'why can't users reach X?' Answer: 'all other traffic is blocked by implicit deny.'
  • ACL is intended as a blacklist (deny bad, allow the rest) but forgets permit any → accidentally blocks everything.
  • Admin adds a deny rule but forgets the permit below it → other traffic breaks.

Rule of thumb: every ACL must have at least one permit and at least one permit that covers the general traffic you want to allow. Whitelist-style ACLs (allow only specific + deny rest) work because of implicit deny. Blacklist-style ACLs (deny specific + allow rest) require explicit permit any.

hack.memory: ACL with ONLY specific permits = everything else blocked silently. Blacklist ACLs need `permit any` at end. Whitelist ACLs rely on implicit deny intentionally. Check every ACL: 'does it have a terminating permit?'
hack.practice: Build the trap deliberately: `access-list 100 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 80`. Apply. Send HTTPS → fails. Send ping → fails. Add `permit ip any any` → both work. Classic lesson in implicit deny.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Very common exam scenario: 'what's broken?' = missing permit any → everything else blocked.

5.6.g.info:
ACLs process packets top to bottom, evaluating each ACE in sequence-number order. The first rule that matches determines the action (permit or deny), and processing stops. No further rules are evaluated.

Consequences:

  • Order is critical — a permit at line 10 beats a deny at line 20 for matching packets, and vice versa.
  • More specific rules go first — you cannot block a specific host if a broader permit above it already matched.
  • Terminal rule is the implicit deny — if no explicit rule matches, the packet is dropped.

Processing example:

ip access-list extended MY_ACL
 10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
 20 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443   ! NEVER REACHED for HTTPS
 30 permit ip any any

Any HTTPS packet matches line 10 and is permitted. Line 20 is unreachable for HTTPS traffic because line 10 caught it first. This is called a shadow rule — a rule that can never be matched because an earlier rule covers the same traffic with a different action.

Another shadow example:

access-list 100 permit ip any any          ! catches everything
access-list 100 deny tcp any any eq 23      ! NEVER REACHED

Telnet is never blocked because line 1 permits all IP traffic first.

Correct ordering pattern:

! Specific denies FIRST
access-list 100 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 23
access-list 100 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 21
! Broad permits AFTER
access-list 100 permit ip any any

How to detect shadow rules: examine each rule in order. For every deny, check if any earlier permit already matches that traffic. For every permit, check if any earlier deny blocks it. Match counters help — shadow rules have 0 matches indefinitely.

hack.memory: Top-down, first match wins, stop evaluating. Order matters. Specific before general. Shadow rules (never reachable) have 0 matches — they're bugs. Implicit deny catches anything unmatched.
hack.practice: Build an ACL with deliberate shadow rule: `permit ip any any` at line 10, `deny tcp any any eq 23` at line 20. Apply, Telnet → still works, line 20 shows 0 matches. Move the deny to line 5 → Telnet blocked. Order matters.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: given packet + ACL, determine result. Trace top-down; first match = result. Ignore rules below the first match.

5.6.g.info:
Specific rules MUST come before general rules when they conflict. This is a direct consequence of top-down, first-match-wins processing. If you want to block one host out of a permitted subnet, the host-specific deny goes first; the subnet-wide permit goes second.

Working example — deny one host, permit the rest of the subnet:

! CORRECT order
ip access-list extended CORRECT
 10 deny ip host 10.0.0.5 any           ! specific first
 20 permit ip 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any    ! general second

Packet from 10.0.0.5 → matches line 10 (deny) → dropped. Packet from 10.0.0.6 → doesn't match line 10 → matches line 20 → permitted.

Broken example — reversed order:

! WRONG order (shadow)
ip access-list extended BROKEN
 10 permit ip 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any    ! general first
 20 deny ip host 10.0.0.5 any           ! NEVER REACHED for 10.0.0.5

Packet from 10.0.0.5 → matches line 10 (permit) → allowed. Line 20 never reached. The intent was to block 10.0.0.5; the actual result is 10.0.0.5 is permitted.

The underlying principle: specific rules are smaller match sets (fewer packets). General rules are larger match sets. If the general set fully contains the specific set, order matters — whichever comes first wins for packets in both sets.

Debugging shadowed rules:

  • Look for zero match counts on rules that should see traffic — often a shadow bug.
  • Walk through the ACL top to bottom for a test packet; the first matching rule determines behavior.
  • Rewrite the ACL with specific rules first, general rules after.

Exam tip: when a question shows an ACL with contradicting rules, trace a test packet top-down and apply the first match. If the 'wrong' rule is hit first, the ACL is in wrong order.

hack.memory: Specific BEFORE general. Always. Deny host before permit subnet. Reversed = shadow bug. Specific rule has smaller match set; put it first or it gets eclipsed.
hack.practice: Deliberately build BROKEN then CORRECT versions of the same intent. Apply each. Test traffic from the specific host. See the behavior differ. Swap once more to reinforce.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: reorder ACEs correctly. Common pitfall.

5.6.g.term: Exam method: trace a packet through an ACL
5.6.g.info:
Many CCNA exam questions present an ACL and a packet, then ask 'what happens to this packet?' The correct method is always the same: trace top-down, find the first match, apply that action, stop reading. Whatever comes after the first match is irrelevant.

Step-by-step trace method:

  1. Identify the packet's attributes — source IP, destination IP, protocol, source port, destination port.
  2. Go to ACE #1 (line 10 typically).
  3. For each attribute required by the ACE, check if the packet matches. All attributes must match for the ACE to match.
  4. If it matches — apply the action (permit or deny). Done. Stop reading.
  5. If it doesn't match — move to the next ACE.
  6. Repeat until a match is found or the implicit deny catches the packet (= denied).

Worked example:

ip access-list extended TEST
 10 permit tcp 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
 20 deny tcp 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
 30 permit ip any any

Question: what happens to a TCP packet from 192.168.1.50 to 10.1.1.100 port 443?

  • ACE 10: protocol TCP ✓, source 192.168.1.50 in 192.168.1.0/24 ✓, dest 10.1.1.100 ✓, dst port 443 ≠ 80 ✗. No match. Continue.
  • ACE 20: protocol TCP ✓, source ✓, dest ✓, dst port 443 ✓. Match! Action: deny. DROPPED. Stop.

Question: what about TCP from 10.99.99.99 to 10.1.1.100 port 80?

  • ACE 10: source 10.99.99.99 not in 192.168.1.0/24. No match.
  • ACE 20: source same miss. No match.
  • ACE 30: protocol IP (matches all IP including TCP) ✓, source any ✓, dest any ✓. Match! Action: permit. ALLOWED.

Common exam traps:

  • Testing an unusual protocol (GRE, OSPF) against ACEs that only match TCP/UDP.
  • Source/destination swapped between ACE and packet.
  • Wildcard mask that looks narrow but is actually broad.
  • Implicit deny catching a packet you expected to pass.

hack.memory: Trace method: identify packet attributes → top-to-bottom check each ACE → all attributes must match → first match wins → stop reading. Unmatched packets hit implicit deny.
hack.practice: 10-packet drill: build an ACL of 5 rules. Throw 10 imaginary packets at it. For each, trace top-down, find the first match, apply action. Check your answer against `show access-lists` counters if labbed. Speed this until it's automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). This trace method answers ~40% of ACL exam questions. Drill to speed.

5.6.h.term: Standard ACL near destination — the full argument
5.6.h.info:
Standard ACLs match only on source IP. Because of this single-criterion matching, placing them near the source would block that source from reaching every destination downstream — even destinations you want them to reach. The fix is to place standard ACLs as close to the destination as possible so the blocking is surgical.

Extended example — three-destination topology:

PC-A (10.0.0.50) connected via R1 — R2 — R3. R3 has three interfaces leading to Server-B (192.168.1.100), Server-C (172.16.1.100), and Server-D (10.99.1.100).

Goal: block PC-A from reaching Server-B only. Allow PC-A to reach Server-C and Server-D.

Near-source placement (R1 inbound) — BAD:

access-list 10 deny host 10.0.0.50
access-list 10 permit any
interface Gi0/0   ! interface facing PC-A
 ip access-group 10 in

Result: PC-A traffic to B, C, and D all die at R1. Over-broad.

Near-destination placement (R3 outbound toward B) — GOOD:

access-list 10 deny host 10.0.0.50
access-list 10 permit any
interface Gi0/1   ! interface facing Server-B
 ip access-group 10 out

Result: PC-A blocked from B only. Paths to C and D are unaffected. Surgical.

Cost of near-destination placement: the blocked traffic still traverses R1 → R2 → R3 → Gi0/1 before being dropped. Bandwidth wasted. For standard ACLs this is acceptable because the alternative (over-broad blocking near source) is worse.

If you need both surgical blocking AND early drop: use an extended ACL. Extended ACLs can match source + destination so they can surgically block specific flows near the source without affecting other destinations.

Exam phrase recognition:

  • 'Place as close to the destination as possible' → standard ACL.
  • 'Place as close to the source as possible' → extended ACL.

hack.memory: Standard → Destination (SD). Source-only matching means source placement over-blocks. Place near destination for surgical control. Cost: wasted bandwidth on the path. Use extended if early drop matters.
hack.practice: Lab the scenario: 3 destinations off R3. Try standard ACL near source (breaks access to ALL). Try near destination (only blocks intended path). Feel the difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 3 (Vol 2). Exam top-5 topic. Standard = Destination is an absolute.

5.6.h.term: Extended ACL near source — the full argument
5.6.h.info:
Extended ACLs match on source + destination + protocol + port (+ flags). This precision lets you surgically block specific flows without affecting anything else from the same source. Because of that precision, you want to drop unwanted packets as early as possible — near the SOURCE — to save bandwidth across the path.

Worked example — block HTTP from a subnet to a server, allow everything else:

Topology: 10.0.0.0/24 users — R1 — R2 — R3 — 192.168.1.100 server. Users should NOT be able to HTTP to 192.168.1.100 but SHOULD be able to HTTPS, SSH, ping, etc.

Near-source placement (R1 inbound) — CORRECT:

access-list 100 deny tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.100 eq 80
access-list 100 permit ip any any
interface Gi0/0   ! facing 10.0.0.0/24 users
 ip access-group 100 in

Result: HTTP packets to 192.168.1.100 die at R1. All other user traffic (HTTPS, SSH, traffic to other servers) passes. Bandwidth on R1-R2 and R2-R3 is not consumed by the doomed HTTP packets.

Near-destination placement (R3 outbound) — WORKS BUT WASTEFUL:

Same ACL, applied on R3 toward the server. Same logical result for users — HTTP blocked — but the unwanted packets traveled across the whole network to be dropped.

Why extended near-source is safe: because the ACL explicitly matches the destination, protocol, and port, only the specific unwanted flow is dropped. Everything else passes untouched. There's no risk of over-blocking — the ACL has enough granularity to distinguish flows.

Combined rule mnemonic:

  • Standard → Destination (SD)
  • Extended → Source (ES)

Repeat: SD / ES. This answers the majority of CCNA ACL-placement questions.

hack.memory: Extended → Source (ES). Granular matching means early drop is safe (no collateral damage). Saves bandwidth across the whole path. Combined: SD + ES.
hack.practice: Same topology as before but with an extended ACL blocking HTTP to a specific server. Near-source version = HTTP dies immediately, other traffic fine. Near-destination version = same logical result but packets waste path bandwidth. Feel the efficiency gain.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Extended = Source is an absolute companion to Standard = Destination.

5.6.h.term: Why placement matters — bandwidth vs collateral damage
5.6.h.info:
ACL placement is a trade-off between two costs: wasted bandwidth (from packets traveling the whole network before being dropped) and collateral damage (from over-blocking legitimate traffic). The SD/ES rules minimize both.

Standard ACLs near source — too much collateral damage: since standard ACLs match only source, placing them near the source blocks that source from reaching any destination. If the admin only wanted to block one destination, this is catastrophic over-blocking.

Standard ACLs near destination — minimal collateral damage, some wasted bandwidth: traffic flows normally across the network and dies at the last-hop router. Bandwidth is wasted between the source and the destination router, but no legitimate traffic is over-blocked.

Extended ACLs near source — minimal both: extended ACLs are precise enough to distinguish specific flows. Near-source placement kills the unwanted flow at the first router, saving bandwidth downstream. Other flows from the same source are unaffected.

Extended ACLs near destination — works but wastes bandwidth: same logical result as near-source extended ACLs, but packets consume bandwidth all the way across the network before being dropped. Functionally equivalent but inefficient.

Putting it together:

  • Standard near destination → surgical but wasteful → only viable option.
  • Standard near source → disaster → over-blocks.
  • Extended near source → surgical + efficient → best.
  • Extended near destination → surgical but wasteful → second-best.

Additional placement factors:

  • Direction (in vs out) — 'in' filters traffic entering the router through that interface; 'out' filters traffic leaving. Choose direction based on where the traffic comes from.
  • Router hardware — high-end routers with CEF-based ACLs suffer minimal performance impact from extra ACLs; small routers with software-based ACLs might prefer placement that minimizes ACL CPU work.
  • Operational visibility — placing an ACL centrally (e.g., at the network edge) makes it easier to manage, even if individual interface placement would be more optimal.

Exam framing: questions may mention both bandwidth and correctness considerations. The SD/ES rule is almost always the right answer for 'where do I place this ACL?' questions.

hack.memory: Placement rule comes from two costs: wasted bandwidth (crossing network before drop) and collateral damage (over-blocking). Standard ACLs only have the latter fix option (near dest). Extended ACLs can have both (near source).
hack.practice: Given a topology, ask yourself: 'where is the ACL type's sweet spot?' Standard = destination only. Extended = source for efficiency. Apply this every time without overthinking.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Memorize SD/ES as one block. Exam tests it relentlessly.

5.6.i.info:
Most CCNA ACL placement questions reduce to three quick steps. Apply them in order:

  1. Read the ACL — is it standard (source only) or extended (source + destination + proto + port)?
  2. Apply the placement rule — Standard → Destination (SD), Extended → Source (ES).
  3. Pick the interface + direction — choose the interface on the chosen-side router that faces the relevant traffic, then pick in or out based on traffic flow.

Step 1 details — identify ACL type:

  • Numbered 1-99 or 1300-1999 → standard.
  • Numbered 100-199 or 2000-2699 → extended.
  • Named standard: ip access-list standard NAME.
  • Named extended: ip access-list extended NAME.
  • Entries match source only → standard. Entries match source + dest + proto + port → extended.

Step 2 details — the rule:

  • Standard → Destination (SD): because standard can only match source, destination-side placement avoids over-blocking other destinations.
  • Extended → Source (ES): because extended can match destination, source-side placement is safe and saves bandwidth.

Step 3 details — direction:

  • Direction is relative to the ROUTER, not the traffic source.
  • in = traffic entering the router through that interface (before routing decision).
  • out = traffic leaving the router through that interface (after routing decision).
  • For source-side placement (extended), apply inbound on the interface facing the source.
  • For destination-side placement (standard), apply outbound on the interface facing the destination.

Worked example:

'Block SSH (TCP 22) from 10.0.0.0/24 to 192.168.1.100. Where do I place it?'

  • Step 1: SSH requires TCP port 22 matching → extended ACL.
  • Step 2: Extended → Source.
  • Step 3: Find the source-side router (the first router 10.0.0.0/24 traffic hits). Apply inbound on the interface facing 10.0.0.0/24.

hack.memory: 3-step method: identify type → apply SD/ES → pick interface + direction. Extended = source = inbound. Standard = destination = outbound.
hack.practice: Drill 5 scenarios: (a) block subnet from reaching one server; (b) block HTTP from subnet to specific server; (c) block single host; (d) block Telnet protocol from subnet; (e) block return traffic. For each, trace the 3 steps. Build reflex.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). The 3-step method handles most ACL placement questions.

5.6.i.term: Scenario question method — parse → classify → place
5.6.i.info:
CCNA scenario questions typically describe: (1) what should be blocked or allowed, (2) a topology, and (3) ask where to place the ACL. Use this parsing method:

Step A: Parse the scenario.

  • What is the SOURCE of the traffic being filtered?
  • What is the DESTINATION?
  • What PROTOCOL and PORT (if specified)?
  • Is the filter a deny (block) or permit (whitelist)?

Step B: Classify the ACL type.

  • Source IP only → standard ACL.
  • Source + dest OR source + proto OR source + port → extended ACL.
  • If the scenario mentions 'block this subnet from reaching this specific server,' you need both source and destination → extended.
  • If the scenario mentions 'block this subnet from accessing anything,' source-only → standard.

Step C: Apply the placement rule.

  • Standard → as close to destination as possible.
  • Extended → as close to source as possible.

Step D: Pick router + interface + direction.

  • Find the router on the chosen side of the topology.
  • Identify the interface that faces the relevant traffic — for source-side, the interface connected toward the source; for destination-side, the interface connected toward the destination.
  • Direction: in for source-facing interface (extended); out for destination-facing interface (standard).

Full worked example:

Scenario: 'Corporate policy requires that users in 10.0.0.0/24 cannot use Telnet (TCP 23) to access any server in the 192.168.1.0/24 subnet. The network is: 10.0.0.0/24 → R1 → R2 → R3 → 192.168.1.0/24. Where and how do you configure the ACL?'

  • Parse: source 10.0.0.0/24, dest 192.168.1.0/24, proto TCP, port 23, deny action.
  • Classify: requires matching dest and port → extended.
  • Placement rule: extended → near source → R1.
  • Direction: on R1's interface facing 10.0.0.0/24, inbound.
  • Configure:
R1(config)# ip access-list extended BLOCK_TELNET
R1(config-ext-nacl)# deny tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 eq 23
R1(config-ext-nacl)# permit ip any any
R1(config)# interface Gi0/0    ! facing 10.0.0.0/24
R1(config-if)# ip access-group BLOCK_TELNET in

hack.memory: 4-step drill: parse (src/dst/proto/port) → classify (std/ext) → place (SD/ES) → config (interface + direction). Practice until the chain is instant.
hack.practice: Take 5 random scenario prompts. Write the parse, classify, place, config steps for each in under 2 minutes per scenario. Exam-speed comes from muscle memory of this chain.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Nearly every ACL scenario question can be solved with this 4-step method.

5.6.j.info:
Applying an ACL as in filters packets entering the router through that interface, before the router makes its routing decision. Packets permitted by the ACL continue to the routing engine; packets denied are dropped immediately.

Processing order for inbound ACL:

  1. Frame arrives on the interface.
  2. Router strips L2 encapsulation, exposing the L3 packet.
  3. Inbound ACL check. If the packet fails the ACL, it's dropped. If it passes, continue.
  4. Routing table lookup → determine egress interface.
  5. (If egress interface has an outbound ACL, that check runs here.)
  6. Re-encapsulate and transmit.

Advantages of inbound:

  • Saves CPU cycles — packets that will be dropped are dropped before the routing lookup, avoiding wasted processing.
  • Saves bandwidth downstream — the packet never exits the router, so it doesn't consume egress-interface bandwidth.
  • Natural for source-based filtering — you know exactly where the traffic is coming from (the interface it entered on).

Configuration:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip access-group 100 in

or for named ACL:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip access-group BLOCK_TELNET in

When to use in:

  • Filtering traffic arriving from a specific network — e.g., incoming from the user LAN, incoming from an untrusted WAN.
  • Extended ACLs near the source — the router's user-facing interface is the natural inbound application point.
  • Anti-spoofing filters at the network edge — drop packets with private source IPs arriving on the internet interface.

hack.memory: `in` = filter BEFORE routing decision. Drop bad packets early. Saves CPU + bandwidth. Natural for source-side filtering. Apply: `ip access-group ACL in` under interface.
hack.practice: Lab: apply ACL inbound on Gi0/0 of R1 (facing 10.0.0.0/24). Capture on Gi0/1 (downstream) — dropped packets never reach it. Move ACL to outbound on Gi0/1 → packets still dropped but wasted the R1 routing cycle.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: know 'in' = before routing. Usually preferred for efficiency.

5.6.j.info:
Applying an ACL as out filters packets leaving the router through that interface, after the router has made its routing decision. The router determines the egress interface first, then checks the outbound ACL before transmitting.

Processing order for outbound ACL:

  1. Frame arrives on some ingress interface.
  2. (If ingress interface has an inbound ACL, that check runs first.)
  3. Router performs routing lookup, determines egress interface.
  4. Outbound ACL check on egress interface. If the packet fails, it's dropped. If it passes, continue.
  5. Re-encapsulate with new L2 and transmit.

Key properties:

  • Routing decision already made — the router knows both the source and destination of the packet, and which interface will egress it.
  • Applies only to transit traffic, not locally-originated traffic. Packets generated BY the router (routing protocol updates, pings from the router, SSH replies) are NOT checked by outbound ACLs. This can be surprising.
  • Costs CPU — the routing lookup already consumed CPU even for packets that will be dropped by the outbound ACL. Inbound filtering is more efficient for 'always drop' cases.

Configuration:

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group 100 out

When to use out:

  • Filtering traffic destined for a specific network regardless of where it came from — e.g., outbound on the interface facing a sensitive server.
  • Standard ACLs near destination — the destination-facing interface outbound is the right place.
  • Consolidating multiple inbound sources — instead of applying the same ACL on 5 inbound interfaces, apply once outbound on the single egress toward the protected resource.

Exam trap: 'which direction do you apply a standard ACL for destination-side placement?' → out. Always out when placing near destination, always in when placing near source.

hack.memory: `out` = filter AFTER routing decision, before transmit. Applies to TRANSIT traffic only, NOT router-generated traffic. Ideal for destination-side placement. Apply: `ip access-group ACL out` under interface.
hack.practice: Apply an ACL outbound on R3's interface facing a server. Send traffic from multiple sources → each is checked once on egress. Try sending ping FROM the router itself (`ping 10.1.1.100`) → outbound ACL does NOT affect router-originated pings. Subtle but important.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: know 'out' = after routing decision. NOT applied to router-originated traffic.

5.6.j.term: ip access-group syntax and one-per-direction rule
5.6.j.info:
The ip access-group command applies an ACL to an interface. It specifies the ACL (by number or name) and the direction (in or out).

Syntax:

interface [TYPE NUMBER]
 ip access-group [ACL-name | ACL-number] {in | out}

Examples:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip access-group 100 in

interface Serial0/0/0
 ip access-group BLOCK_TELNET out

One ACL per direction per protocol per interface:

  • You CAN have one IPv4 inbound ACL AND one IPv4 outbound ACL on the same interface simultaneously.
  • You CANNOT have two IPv4 inbound ACLs on the same interface — the second overwrites the first.
  • IPv4 and IPv6 ACLs are independent — you can have IPv4 in + IPv4 out + IPv6 in + IPv6 out on the same interface (4 total ACLs per interface).

Removing an ACL from an interface:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 no ip access-group 100 in

Verifying application:

Router# show ip interface Gi0/0
GigabitEthernet0/0 is up, line protocol is up
  Internet address is 10.0.0.1/24
  Inbound access list is 100
  Outgoing access list is not set

Look for 'Inbound access list' and 'Outgoing access list' lines. 'Not set' means no ACL in that direction.

Common mistake: forgetting to specify direction. ip access-group 100 without in/out is a syntax error. Always include direction.

hack.memory: Syntax: `ip access-group ACL {in|out}` under an interface. Max: 1 IPv4 in + 1 IPv4 out per interface. Remove with `no ip access-group ACL direction`. Verify with `show ip interface`.
hack.practice: Apply 2 ACLs to same interface (one in, one out) — works. Try to apply second `ip access-group 200 in` over the existing `ip access-group 100 in` — the second replaces the first. Verify with `show ip interface`.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: know syntax exactly. One ACL per direction per protocol per interface.

5.6.j.info:
A frequent confusion: direction is always relative to the ROUTER, never to the end user or the traffic source. Get this wrong and you'll flip the direction in every question.

The rule:

  • in = traffic entering the router through this interface. Arrow points AT the router on this interface.
  • out = traffic leaving the router through this interface. Arrow points AWAY from the router on this interface.

Concrete example:

R1 has Gi0/0 facing LAN-A (10.0.0.0/24) and Gi0/1 facing LAN-B (192.168.1.0/24). A user in LAN-A (10.0.0.50) pings a host in LAN-B (192.168.1.100).

  • Echo request packet: LAN-A → R1 Gi0/0 (inbound on Gi0/0) → routing → R1 Gi0/1 (outbound on Gi0/1) → LAN-B.
  • Echo reply packet: LAN-B → R1 Gi0/1 (inbound on Gi0/1) → routing → R1 Gi0/0 (outbound on Gi0/0) → LAN-A.

To filter the echo request at R1, you have two options:

  • Apply inbound on Gi0/0 (before routing) — traffic dies immediately.
  • Apply outbound on Gi0/1 (after routing) — traffic reaches Gi0/1 and is then dropped before transmission.

Both filter the same traffic; the difference is when in the router's processing the drop occurs.

Practical drawing trick: sketch the router as a box. For each interface, draw two arrows: one pointing INTO the box (in) and one pointing OUT of the box (out). Label 'in' and 'out' on those arrows. This visual makes direction unambiguous.

Common confusion patterns:

  • Students think 'out' means 'leaving the user.' Wrong — it's from the router's perspective.
  • Students think an inbound ACL on a WAN interface should filter traffic going OUT to the internet. Wrong — inbound filters traffic coming IN from the internet.
  • Students confuse 'inbound' with 'incoming to the destination.' Wrong — it's about which side of the router the interface is.

hack.memory: Direction = ROUTER's view. `in` = entering the router. `out` = leaving the router. Always draw arrows on the router box. User's perspective is irrelevant.
hack.practice: Draw a router with two interfaces and a user on each side. Label every traffic flow with two arrows (direction relative to the router). Then place ACLs inbound/outbound and trace what each filters. This visual cements direction forever.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam: direction relative to router. Many students flip this — don't be one of them.

5.6.k.term: access-class — applying ACL to VTY lines
5.6.k.info:
To restrict which source IPs can remotely manage a Cisco device via VTY lines (SSH/Telnet), use access-class — NOT ip access-group. This is one of the most tested distinctions in ACL configuration.

Why a separate command for VTY?

  • VTY lines are virtual, not physical — they don't have an interface-level configuration in the traditional sense.
  • VTY ACLs control who can establish a management session to the device (SSH, Telnet). This is different from controlling transit traffic (which uses ip access-group).
  • access-class always filters SOURCE only, so a standard ACL is almost always appropriate.

Configuration:

! Step 1 — build the ACL
access-list 5 permit host 10.0.0.50
access-list 5 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255
! (implicit deny any — rest denied)

! Step 2 — apply to VTY lines
line vty 0 15
 access-class 5 in
 transport input ssh

Now only 10.0.0.50 and anyone in 192.168.1.0/24 can SSH to this device. All other sources are silently dropped at the VTY.

Direction for access-class: nearly always in. Outbound would filter sessions the router initiates, which is rarely useful. Expect access-class X in.

Best practice pattern:

  • Use a standard ACL (numbered 1-99 or named standard) — you only need source IP.
  • Permit a small admin subnet or specific admin IPs.
  • Let the implicit deny catch everything else.
  • Apply inbound on line vty 0 15 (covering all 16 VTY lines).
  • Also configure transport input ssh to force SSH-only (not Telnet).

Exam scenario: 'How do you restrict SSH access to specific IPs on a Cisco router?' → build a standard ACL + apply with access-class ACL in on line vty 0 15.

hack.memory: `access-class ACL in` on `line vty 0 15` = restrict management access by source IP. Use a standard ACL (source only). Always `in`. Implicit deny locks out everyone not permitted.
hack.practice: Lab: `access-list 5 permit host 10.0.0.50` → `line vty 0 15` → `access-class 5 in`. SSH from 10.0.0.50 → works. SSH from 192.168.99.x → rejected by access-class. Verify with `show line vty 0` and debug.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Exam: 'restrict SSH by source IP' → access-class + standard ACL + line vty 0 15.

5.6.k.term: access-group vs access-class — the critical distinction
5.6.k.info:
These two commands sound similar and are both used with ACLs, but they do fundamentally different things. Confusing them is a common mistake and a frequent exam trap.

ip access-group — applied on an interface. Filters transit traffic (traffic flowing through the router, not to the router's own management plane).

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip access-group 100 in
 ip access-group 101 out

access-class — applied on a VTY line. Filters remote management sessions (SSH/Telnet connecting to the device itself).

line vty 0 15
 access-class 5 in

Direct comparison:

Aspectip access-groupaccess-class
Applied whereInterfaceLine (VTY typically)
Filters whatTransit traffic through routerManagement access to router
Typical ACL typeStandard or extendedStandard (source only)
Directionsin or outin (almost always)
Use case'Block HTTP from subnet A to server B''Only these IPs can SSH to the router'

Exam trap: a question might show ip access-group 5 in under line vty 0 15 — this is invalid syntax. VTY lines need access-class. Similarly, access-class 100 in under an interface is invalid.

Memory hook: 'CLASS is for command-line access (CLI).' 'GROUP is for group of transit traffic.' Two very different purposes, similar-sounding names — don't mix them up.

hack.memory: `ip access-group` = interface, transit traffic. `access-class` = VTY line, management access. CLASS = CLI access. GROUP = transit group. Never mix them up.
hack.practice: Apply both correctly on a test router: `ip access-group 100 in` on an interface + `access-class 5 in` on VTY. Then deliberately swap them (put access-class on interface) → IOS rejects the command. That hands-on error makes the distinction concrete.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Classic exam trap. Memorize: interface → ip access-group. VTY → access-class.

5.6.k.info:
Beyond just using access-class, there are a handful of recurring mistakes that show up in exam questions and real-world deployments.

Pitfall 1: locking yourself out. If you build an ACL that doesn't include your own management IP and apply it to VTY, your next login attempt will be rejected — from your own workstation. Always test from the intended admin subnet first, and have a console cable or out-of-band access ready.

Pitfall 2: forgetting transport input. Even with a strong access-class, if transport input telnet is still configured, someone in your allowed subnet can Telnet (cleartext). Always explicitly set transport input ssh to force SSH.

line vty 0 15
 access-class ADMIN_ACL in
 transport input ssh
 login local
 exec-timeout 5 0

Pitfall 3: missing local user account. SSH requires local authentication. Configure username admin secret STRONG_PASSWORD and login local on the VTY lines. Also configure ip domain-name and generate RSA keys (crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048) before SSH will work.

Pitfall 4: relying on VTY ACL alone. access-class only controls who can SSH. It does NOT prevent network reconnaissance — attackers can still ping, traceroute, or scan the device. For full edge protection, combine VTY ACLs with an inbound ACL on the external interface that explicitly denies SSH from untrusted sources at the IP layer.

Pitfall 5: not permitting the loopback / management VLAN. Many Cisco devices are managed via a loopback or management VLAN. Make sure your access-class ACL permits those sources too, or the device becomes unreachable from intended sources.

Best-practice VTY hardening checklist:

  • access-class with specific admin source IPs.
  • transport input ssh (no Telnet).
  • login local or AAA-authenticated login.
  • Strong password / SSH keys.
  • exec-timeout 5 0 (auto-logout after 5 min idle).
  • login block-for ... attempts ... within ... for brute-force protection.
  • Syslog all login attempts.

hack.memory: VTY hardening: access-class + transport input ssh + login local + strong cred + exec-timeout + login block-for. Always have console/out-of-band access in case you lock yourself out.
hack.practice: Lab the full VTY hardening: build ACL, apply access-class, set transport input ssh, configure username + login local, set exec-timeout, enable login block-for. Test from permitted + denied sources. Have console cable ready.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Real-world critical skill. Exam covers `access-class` + `transport input`.

5.6.l.term: Surgical edit — delete an ACE with `no [seq#]`
5.6.l.info:
Modern IOS lets you delete individual ACEs by sequence number without removing and recreating the entire ACL. This is the core benefit of named ACLs with sequence numbers.

Procedure:

  1. Identify the sequence number of the entry to delete using show access-lists NAME.
  2. Enter the named-ACL sub-config mode: ip access-list {standard|extended} NAME.
  3. Type no [seq#].
  4. Exit.

Example:

Router# show access-lists MY_ACL
Extended IP access list MY_ACL
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
    20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
    30 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 23
    40 permit ip any any

Router(config)# ip access-list extended MY_ACL
Router(config-ext-nacl)# no 30
Router(config-ext-nacl)# exit

Router# show access-lists MY_ACL
Extended IP access list MY_ACL
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
    20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
    40 permit ip any any

Entry 30 is gone; entries 10, 20, and 40 are untouched.

Why this matters:

  • Before sequence-number editing, any change required no access-list N (deletes entire ACL) → rebuild → reapply — which briefly left the interface unprotected.
  • With sequence editing, the ACL stays applied throughout — no gap in protection.
  • Also reduces the risk of typos in rebuilding a long ACL.

Numbered ACLs: modern IOS (~12.2+) also supports sequence-number editing on numbered ACLs. You enter ip access-list extended 100 (use the number as the 'name') and then use no [seq#] like with named ACLs.

Exam framing: 'how do you delete one entry from an ACL without removing the whole ACL?' → no [seq#] inside the named-ACL sub-config.

hack.memory: Delete one ACE: enter named-ACL sub-config → `no [seq#]`. Keeps ACL applied during edit, avoids the old 'delete-all-and-rebuild' problem. Works on named AND modern numbered ACLs.
hack.practice: Build a 4-entry named ACL. `no 20` to delete the second entry. Verify other entries untouched. Show running config — the ACL still has entries 10, 30, 40. That's surgical editing.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Guaranteed exam question. Know the exact command.

5.6.l.term: Insert an ACE at a specific sequence number
5.6.l.info:
You can insert a new ACE between existing ones by typing the desired sequence number at the start of the new rule. IOS places the rule at that sequence number, between whatever lower and higher entries already exist.

Example:

! Current ACL (entries at 10, 20, 30)
Extended IP access list MY_ACL
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
    20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
    30 permit ip any any

! Want to insert a deny at sequence 15 (between 10 and 20)
Router(config)# ip access-list extended MY_ACL
Router(config-ext-nacl)# 15 deny tcp 192.168.99.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.1.1.100
Router(config-ext-nacl)# exit

! Verify
Router# show access-lists MY_ACL
Extended IP access list MY_ACL
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443
    15 deny tcp 192.168.99.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.1.1.100
    20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80
    30 permit ip any any

Sequence 15 now sits between 10 and 20, exactly where we wanted it.

Important constraints:

  • If you try to insert at a sequence number that's already taken, IOS replaces the existing rule at that number.
  • If you run out of gaps (e.g., you've filled 11-19 with inserts), use ip access-list resequence to open up space.
  • Typing just permit ... without a sequence number appends at the end using auto-numbered increments (next multiple of 10).

Best practice: when initially building an ACL, use increments of 10 or 20 so there's room between entries for future insertions. If you need finer granularity, resequence first.

Exam framing: 'how do you add a rule between existing entries?' → type the specific sequence number + rule inside the named-ACL sub-config.

hack.memory: Insert an ACE: type `[seq#] permit|deny ...` inside named-ACL sub-config. IOS places it at that sequence. If seq# taken, it replaces. Leave gaps (default increments of 10) for future inserts.
hack.practice: Create a 3-entry ACL at sequences 10, 20, 30. Insert at 15 → lands between 10 and 20. Insert at 25 → between 20 and 30. Try to insert again at 15 — the existing entry is replaced.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Know insert syntax and constraint: specify seq# to place between existing entries.

5.6.l.info:
The ip access-list resequence command renumbers all ACEs in an ACL from a new starting point with a new increment. Use it to clean up after many inserts have filled the gaps, or to standardize numbering across multiple ACLs.

Syntax:

ip access-list resequence [NAME | number] [start] [increment]

Examples:

  • ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 10 10 — start at 10, increment by 10 (default layout).
  • ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 100 5 — start at 100, increment by 5 (dense numbering).
  • ip access-list resequence 100 10 20 — numbered ACL 100, start 10, increment 20.

Before/after:

! Messy (after many inserts):
 10 permit tcp ...
 11 permit tcp ...
 12 permit tcp ...
 13 deny tcp ...
 20 permit ip any any

! Run: ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 10 10

! Result (clean):
 10 permit tcp ...
 20 permit tcp ...
 30 permit tcp ...
 40 deny tcp ...
 50 permit ip any any

Key properties:

  • Preserves order and content — only sequence numbers change. The ACL logic is identical before and after.
  • Preserves ACL application — resequencing does not remove the ACL from interfaces or affect running traffic.
  • Useful before handoff — making the sequence numbers clean makes the ACL easier for another admin to read.

Workflow tip:

  1. Build ACL with auto sequence 10, 20, 30...
  2. Over time, insert entries at 15, 17, 18, 25.
  3. You need to insert one more between 17 and 18 → no room.
  4. Run ip access-list resequence MY_ACL 10 10 → now gaps are available again.
  5. Insert the new rule at a fresh sequence number.

Exam framing: know the command syntax and purpose. 'How do you renumber an ACL's sequence numbers?' → ip access-list resequence NAME START INCREMENT.

hack.memory: `ip access-list resequence NAME START INCREMENT` = renumber all ACEs. Preserves order + content. Does not disrupt applied ACLs. Use after many inserts to restore gaps.
hack.practice: Build an ACL, insert entries until gaps run out (10, 11, 12, 13, 20). Try to insert at 14 — no gap. Resequence to 10/10 → now numbered 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Gaps restored.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Know command syntax: `ip access-list resequence NAME START INCREMENT`.

5.6.m.term: show access-lists — the main verification command
5.6.m.info:
show access-lists is the primary ACL verification command. It displays ALL ACLs configured on the device (IPv4, IPv6, MAC) with sequence numbers and match counters. Match counters are essential for troubleshooting — they tell you which rules are actually matching traffic.

Output example:

Router# show access-lists
Standard IP access list 5
    10 permit host 10.0.0.50 (15 matches)
    20 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 (342 matches)

Extended IP access list WEB_FILTER
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 (8734 matches)
    20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80 (120 matches)
    30 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 23 (5 matches)
    40 permit ip any any (1500 matches)

Reading the output:

  • ACL type + identifier (Standard 5, Extended WEB_FILTER).
  • Each ACE with sequence number + action + criteria.
  • Match count in parentheses — how many packets hit that rule since last counter reset.

Filtering the output:

  • show access-lists NAME — shows only the named ACL.
  • show access-lists 100 — shows only ACL 100.
  • show access-lists | include [pattern] — pipe through include/exclude for long ACLs.

Clearing match counters:

Router# clear ip access-list counters [NAME | number]

Clears the (N matches) counts. Useful when you want to observe fresh traffic without old counts mixed in.

Troubleshooting workflow with match counters:

  • Counter at 0 on a rule that should see traffic → rule is shadowed by an earlier rule, or the ACL isn't applied, or traffic isn't flowing.
  • Counter incrementing on a deny rule → traffic is being dropped; check syslog if log keyword is set.
  • Counter on implicit deny (if made explicit) shows how much traffic is hitting the catch-all.

Exam framing: expect to interpret show access-lists output. Identify sequence, action, criteria, match count. Common questions: 'which rule is catching the blocked traffic?' — look at deny rules with increasing counters.

hack.memory: `show access-lists` = all ACLs + sequence numbers + match counters. Filter by name/number. Clear counters with `clear ip access-list counters`. Match counts are troubleshooting gold.
hack.practice: On a lab router with an applied ACL, run `show access-lists`. Generate traffic that matches different entries — watch counters increment. Run `clear ip access-list counters` — counters reset to 0. Generate traffic again — watch the new counts.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Exam interprets show output. Know: sequence, action, criteria, match count.

5.6.m.term: show ip interface — where is the ACL applied?
5.6.m.info:
show ip interface [interface] reveals the IP-layer configuration of an interface, including which ACLs are applied and in which direction. This is essential for verifying that an ACL is actually attached where you think it is.

Output example (abbreviated):

Router# show ip interface Gi0/0
GigabitEthernet0/0 is up, line protocol is up
  Internet address is 10.0.0.1/24
  Broadcast address is 255.255.255.255
  Address determined by non-volatile memory
  MTU is 1500 bytes
  Helper address is not set
  Directed broadcast forwarding is disabled
  Outgoing access list is not set
  Inbound  access list is WEB_FILTER
  Proxy ARP is enabled
  ...

Key lines to look for:

  • Inbound access list is [NAME/number] — ACL applied in.
  • Outgoing access list is [NAME/number] — ACL applied out.
  • 'not set' on either line means no ACL in that direction.

Short form: show ip interface brief shows one line per interface (IP, status) without ACL info. Use show ip interface without brief for the detailed view.

Troubleshooting workflow:

  1. show access-lists → verify the ACL exists and has the correct entries.
  2. show ip interface → verify the ACL is applied to the correct interface in the correct direction.
  3. If either is wrong, fix it. If both are right but traffic still misbehaves, check routing (show ip route), interface status (show ip interface brief), and match counters for zero-match anomalies.

Common pitfall: ACL is configured but not applied anywhere. show access-lists shows the ACL; show ip interface on the intended interface shows 'not set' for the direction. Remedy: ip access-group NAME direction under the interface.

Another pitfall: ACL applied in wrong direction. Outbound instead of inbound (or vice versa) — traffic flows past without being checked. Verify the direction carefully.

left.label: show ip interface Gi0/0
items: Outgoing access list is not set

hack.memory: `show ip interface INT` = which ACLs are applied where. Look for 'Inbound access list' and 'Outgoing access list'. 'not set' = no ACL. Essential troubleshooting step.
hack.practice: Apply an ACL to a test interface. `show ip interface` → confirms ACL is attached. Remove it. Re-run `show ip interface` → confirms removal. Practice this verify loop after every ACL change.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 3-4 (Vol 2). Primary troubleshooting step. Know exactly what lines to read.

5.6.m.term: show ip access-lists — IPv4 filter, same format
5.6.m.info:
show ip access-lists is similar to show access-lists but filters the output to IPv4 ACLs only. On devices that also have IPv6 ACLs (ipv6 access-list) or MAC ACLs (mac access-list), this command hides everything but IPv4.

Output example:

Router# show ip access-lists
Standard IP access list 5
    10 permit host 10.0.0.50 (15 matches)
    20 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 (342 matches)

Extended IP access list WEB_FILTER
    10 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 443 (8734 matches)
    20 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 80 (120 matches)
    30 deny tcp any host 10.1.1.100 eq 23 (5 matches)
    40 permit ip any any (1500 matches)

Identical format to show access-lists — same sequence + criteria + match count — just filtered to IPv4.

Related commands:

  • show access-lists — all ACLs (IPv4 + IPv6 + MAC).
  • show ip access-lists — IPv4 only.
  • show ipv6 access-list — IPv6 only.
  • show mac access-list — L2 MAC ACLs.

When to prefer ip access-lists: on devices with many ACL types or where you know the issue is IPv4-specific. Otherwise show access-lists is the default.

Combined verification flow:

show ip access-lists                    ! the ACL content + matches
show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/0   ! where is it applied?
show running-config | include access   ! defined and applied across all interfaces/VTY

The third command is powerful — show running-config | include access greps the config for any line containing 'access', which captures both ACL definitions and ip access-group / access-class applications across all interfaces and VTY lines. Great for a complete audit.

Exam framing: know the variations of show commands and what each reveals. A troubleshooting question might test whether you pick the right command for the symptom.

hack.memory: `show ip access-lists` = IPv4 ACL content + matches. Same format as `show access-lists` but IPv4-filtered. Pair with `show ip interface` to verify both content AND placement.
hack.practice: Run all three commands on a lab with applied ACLs: `show access-lists`, `show ip access-lists`, `show ip interface`. Compare output. On a mixed IPv4+IPv6 device, watch how `show ip access-lists` filters out IPv6.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 33-35. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 (Vol 2). Know the variations — exam may test command choice.
